nodes.label: Abbas-Ali Kadkhodaei
nodes.role: Jurist member of the Guardian Council; former spokesman

data.role: Jurist member of the Guardian Council; former spokesman
data.bio: Abbas-Ali Kadkhodaei is a jurist member of the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) who served as the Council's public face as spokesman for many years before [Hadi Tahan Nazif](/people/hadi-tahan-nazif) took on that role. Appointed by Secretary [Ahmad Jannati](/people/ahmad-jannati) to the Central Committee for Election Supervision, he was the primary official voice defending the Council's candidate disqualifications. Before the [2020 parliamentary election](/events/parliamentary-election-iran-2020), he warned that 'insults or inflammatory remarks towards the Supreme Leader' would be considered during candidate vetting. He was sanctioned by the US Treasury in February 2020.

nodes.label: Abbas Araghchi
nodes.role: Minister of Foreign Affairs (2024–present); lead nuclear negotiator

data.role: Minister of Foreign Affairs (2024–present); lead nuclear negotiator
data.bio: Abbas Araghchi is Iran's current Foreign Minister under [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian). A career diplomat, he served as Deputy Foreign Minister for Legal and International Affairs and was Iran's lead technical nuclear negotiator during the JCPOA talks under [Mohammad Javad Zarif](/people/javad-zarif). He was one of the architects of the 2015 nuclear deal. After the deal's collapse he remained at the [Ministry of Foreign Affairs](/orgs/ministry-of-foreign-affairs) as a senior official. His appointment as FM under Pezeshkian was seen as a signal of continuity with the reformist-era foreign policy school. His early tenure has been shaped by the post-2026 succession crisis and Iran's recalibrated position under [Mojtaba Khamenei](/people/mojtaba-khamenei).

nodes.label: Abdolnaser Hemmati
nodes.role: Former Finance Minister (2024, briefly); former Central Bank Governor (2018–2021)

data.role: Former Finance Minister (2024, briefly); former Central Bank Governor (2018–2021)
data.bio: Abdolnaser Hemmati is an economist who served as Governor of the Central Bank of Iran (2018–2021) and ran as a presidential candidate in the 2021 election. [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) appointed him Finance Minister in August 2024, but he resigned in March 2025. As Central Bank Governor he navigated the severe economic pressure of US sanctions after 2018. His 2021 presidential candidacy was approved by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council), making him one of the more moderate candidates allowed to run.

nodes.label: Abolhassan Banisadr
nodes.role: 1st President of the Islamic Republic of Iran (1980–1981)

data.role: 1st President of the Islamic Republic of Iran (1980–1981)
data.bio: Abolhassan Banisadr was the first president of the Islamic Republic, elected in January 1980 with 76% of the vote. An economist and political thinker who had been close to [Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) in exile, he quickly came into conflict with the clerical establishment and the [Islamic Republican Party](/orgs/islamic-republican-party). He was impeached by parliament in June 1981 and fled to France, where he lived in exile until his death in 2021.

nodes.label: Ahmad Alam al-Huda
nodes.role: Friday Prayer Imam of Mashhad; representative of Supreme Leader in Razavi Khorasan

data.role: Friday Prayer Imam of Mashhad; representative of Supreme Leader in Razavi Khorasan
data.bio: Ahmad Alam al-Huda is the hardline Friday Prayer Imam of Mashhad, appointed by [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) as his personal representative in Razavi Khorasan Province. He is the father-in-law of [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) — Raisi married his daughter Jamileh Alamolhoda. He supervised Saeed Jalili's doctoral dissertation at Imam Sadeq University, and his sermons from Mashhad's Friday prayer platform have long served as a barometer of the hardliner wing's positions on domestic and foreign policy.

nodes.label: Ahmad Hosseini Khorasani
nodes.role: Clerical member of the Guardian Council

data.role: Clerical member of the Guardian Council
data.bio: Ahmad Hosseini Khorasani is a clerical member of the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council), appointed by [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei). He is one of the six Islamic jurists (faqihs) on the Council responsible for assessing legislation's conformity with Islamic law.

nodes.label: Ahmad Jannati
nodes.role: Secretary of the Guardian Council (1992–present); former Chairman of Assembly of Experts

data.role: Secretary of the Guardian Council (1992–present); former Chairman of Assembly of Experts
data.bio: Ahmad Jannati has been a member of the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) since its founding in 1980, and its secretary since 1992 — the longest-tenured figure in any constitutional body of the Islamic Republic. A founding member of the hardline [Haghani Seminary](/orgs/haghani-seminary) in Qom alongside [Mohammad-Taqi Mesbah Yazdi](/people/mohammad-taqi-mesbah-yazdi), he is among the most extreme voices in Iranian politics, repeatedly calling for the execution of political opponents and systematically disqualifying reformist candidates. He served concurrently as Chairman of the [Assembly of Experts](/orgs/assembly-of-experts). His continuation in office into his late nineties has been attributed to political calculation over capacity.

nodes.label: Ahmad Khatami
nodes.role: Member of Guardian Council; substitute Friday Prayer Imam of Tehran (2005–present)

data.role: Member of Guardian Council; substitute Friday Prayer Imam of Tehran (2005–present)
data.bio: Ahmad Khatami is a hardline Shia cleric and member of the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) and the [Assembly of Experts](/orgs/assembly-of-experts). He shares a surname with reformist President [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami) but is entirely unrelated — a fact that gave him national prominence in 1997. He was appointed substitute Friday Prayer Imam of Tehran in 2005 by [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei), becoming the youngest person to hold that position. During the [2009 Green Movement protests](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) he declared the protesters to be engaging in *mohareb* (war against God), a capital offence, and called on the judiciary to execute opposition leaders [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi) and [Mehdi Karroubi](/people/mehdi-karroubi) as 'leaders of sedition'. Appointed to the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) by [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) in 2020 to replace Mohammad Yazdi. He was mentioned as a candidate for Supreme Leader succession but reportedly opposed [Mojtaba Khamenei](/people/mojtaba-khamenei)'s elevation in the Assembly of Experts.

nodes.label: Ahmad Vahidi
nodes.role: Former Minister of Interior (2021–2024); former Minister of Defense (2009–2013)

data.role: Former Minister of Interior (2021–2024); former Minister of Defense (2009–2013)
data.bio: Ahmad Vahidi is one of the most internationally wanted figures in the Iranian establishment. A founding member of the [IRGC Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force) and its early commander, he was later appointed Defense Minister under [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) (2009–2013). Under [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) he became Interior Minister (2021–2024), supervising Iran's domestic security apparatus and election administration. Argentina issued an Interpol red notice against Vahidi for alleged planning of the 1994 AMIA bombing in Buenos Aires that killed 85 people. He is subject to US and EU sanctions. He is a Brigadier General of the IRGC.

nodes.label: Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani
nodes.role: 4th President of Iran (1989–1997); Chairman of Expediency Discernment Council (1989–2017)

data.role: 4th President of Iran (1989–1997); Chairman of Expediency Discernment Council (1989–2017)
data.bio: Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani was one of the most powerful figures in post-revolutionary Iran. He served as Speaker of the [Islamic Consultative Assembly](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) from 1980 to 1989 and as president from 1989 to 1997. A cleric and confidant of [Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini), he is associated with the pragmatist wing of Iranian politics and championed post-war economic reconstruction. He chaired the [Expediency Discernment Council](/orgs/expediency-discernment-council) until his death in January 2017.

nodes.label: Ali Akbar Nategh-Nouri
nodes.role: Speaker of Parliament (1992–2000); Head of Supreme Leader's Inspection Office

data.role: Speaker of Parliament (1992–2000); Head of Supreme Leader's Inspection Office
data.bio: Ali Akbar Nategh-Nouri served as Speaker of Parliament for two full terms, 1992 to 2000. He was the establishment-backed frontrunner in the 1997 presidential election before being unexpectedly defeated by reformist [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami) in a landslide. He subsequently served as head of the Inspection [Office of the Supreme Leader](/orgs/office-of-the-supreme-leader)'s office. He represents the traditional conservative wing of Iranian politics.

nodes.label: Ali Akbar Salehi
nodes.role: Former Minister of Foreign Affairs (2010–2013); former head of Atomic Energy Organisation (2009–2013, 2015–2021)

data.role: Former Minister of Foreign Affairs (2010–2013); former head of Atomic Energy Organisation (2009–2013, 2015–2021)
data.bio: Ali Akbar Salehi is a nuclear physicist and diplomat who served as [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad)'s Foreign Minister (2010–2013) after [Manouchehr Mottaki](/people/manouchehr-mottaki) was abruptly dismissed. He simultaneously headed the Atomic Energy Organisation of Iran (AEOI). He led Iran's nuclear delegation in early negotiations with world powers. He was Iran's ambassador to the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) 2006–2009. Under [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani) he returned as AEOI head (2015–2021), overseeing the nuclear programme through the JCPOA period and its collapse. He is sanctioned by the US.

nodes.label: Ali Akbar Velayati
nodes.role: Foreign Policy Adviser to the Supreme Leader (1997–present); former Foreign Minister (1981–1997)

data.role: Foreign Policy Adviser to the Supreme Leader (1997–present); former Foreign Minister (1981–1997)
data.bio: Ali Akbar Velayati is a physician-turned-diplomat who served as Iran's Foreign Minister for over fifteen years — the longest tenure in the Islamic Republic's history — spanning the prime ministership of [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi), the presidency of [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei), and two terms of [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani). Since 1997 he has been [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei)'s personal adviser on international affairs, making him arguably more influential than many sitting foreign ministers. An Argentine court issued an international arrest warrant against him in 2006 for his alleged role in planning the 1994 AMIA bombing in Buenos Aires that killed 85 people, and the US Treasury sanctioned him in 2019. He is a member of the [Expediency Discernment Council](/orgs/expediency-discernment-council) and head of the board of the Islamic Azad University.

nodes.label: Ali-Asghar Badizadegan
nodes.role: Co-founder of Mojahedin-e Khalq (MEK)

data.role: Co-founder of Mojahedin-e Khalq (MEK)
data.bio: Ali-Asghar Badizadegan was an engineer and one of the three co-founders of the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq) alongside [Mohammad Hanifnejad](/people/mohammad-hanifnejad) and [Saeed Mohsen](/people/saeed-mohsen). He helped establish the MEK's intellectual foundations, blending progressive Islamic thought with revolutionary theory. He was arrested by SAVAK in 1971 and executed on 25 May 1972.

nodes.label: Ali Fadavi
nodes.role: Former IRGC Deputy Commander-in-Chief (2019–2025); former IRGC Navy Commander (2010–2018)

data.role: Former IRGC Deputy Commander-in-Chief (2019–2025); former IRGC Navy Commander (2010–2018)
data.bio: Ali Fadavi commanded the IRGC Navy from 2010 to 2018, then became Deputy Commander-in-Chief of the entire [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) from 2019. A veteran of the Iran–Iraq War with an intelligence background, he was known for bellicose rhetoric against the US Navy and repeatedly threatened to destroy American vessels in the Persian Gulf. In January 2016 he received the Fath Medal from [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) for arresting ten US Navy sailors in the Persian Gulf. He was replaced as IRGC Navy commander by [Alireza Tangsiri](/people/alireza-tangsiri) in 2018. He was sanctioned by the US, EU, and multiple allied governments.

nodes.label: Ali Fallahian
nodes.role: Former Minister of Intelligence (1989–1997)

data.role: Former Minister of Intelligence (1989–1997)
data.bio: Ali Fallahian served as Iran's Minister of Intelligence under [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani) for the full eight years of his presidency (1989–1997). He is widely believed to have directed Iran's covert assassination programme against dissidents abroad during this period, including the 1992 Mykonos restaurant murders in Berlin — for which a German court in 1997 issued a warrant naming Fallahian — and the 1994 AMIA bombing in Buenos Aires. A second German arrest warrant was issued in 1996. He was replaced when [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami) took office. He later served in the [Assembly of Experts](/orgs/assembly-of-experts) and has been subject to international sanctions by the EU and US.

nodes.label: Ali Khamenei
nodes.role: 2nd Supreme Leader of the Islamic Republic of Iran (1989–2026)

data.role: 2nd Supreme Leader of the Islamic Republic of Iran (1989–2026)
data.bio: Ali Khamenei served as Iran's third president from 1981 to 1989, then succeeded [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) as Supreme Leader following Khomeini's death. As Supreme Leader for nearly four decades, he exercised ultimate authority over all branches of government, the military, and the judiciary. He oversaw Iran's nuclear programme, the crackdown on the [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) in 2009, and the response to the Mahsa Amini protests in 2022. He died on 28 February 2026.

nodes.label: Ali Larijani
nodes.role: Speaker of Parliament (2008–2020); Former chief nuclear negotiator (d. 2026)

data.role: Speaker of Parliament (2008–2020); Former chief nuclear negotiator (d. 2026)
data.bio: Ali Larijani was the longest-serving speaker in the history of the Islamic Republic, holding the position from 2008 to 2020. He previously served as Iran's chief nuclear negotiator (2005–2007) and secretary of the [Supreme National Security Council](/orgs/supreme-national-security-council). He was disqualified from the 2021 presidential election by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council). His brother [Sadegh Larijani](/people/sadegh-larijani) served as head of the judiciary. He was killed on 17 March 2026 in an [Israeli airstrike on Tehran](/events/larijani-assassination-2026), along with his son Morteza Larijani.

nodes.label: Ali Motahari
nodes.role: Second Deputy Speaker of Parliament, 10th Majlis (2016–2019); MP for Tehran (2008–2020)

data.role: Second Deputy Speaker of Parliament, 10th Majlis (2016–2019); MP for Tehran (2008–2020)
data.bio: Ali Motahari is the son of the martyred Islamic scholar [Morteza Motahhari](/people/morteza-motahhari) and the brother-in-law of former [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) Speaker [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani). He represented Tehran for three consecutive terms (8th–10th Majlis, 2008–2020), serving as Second Deputy Speaker in the 10th Majlis (2016–2019). A principlist by formal affiliation, he was widely regarded as a maverick who publicly challenged [Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad), criticised [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) political interference, and called for the release of [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) leaders. He was disqualified by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) from the 2020 elections. He holds a PhD in philosophy from the University of Tehran.

nodes.label: Ali Nikzad
nodes.role: Second Deputy Speaker, 11th and 12th Majlis (2020–present)

data.role: Second Deputy Speaker, 11th and 12th Majlis (2020–present)
data.bio: Ali Nikzad is a conservative civil engineer and politician from Ardabil Province who has served as Second Deputy Speaker of the [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) under Ghalibaf across two consecutive sessions (2020–present). He previously served as Minister of Roads and Urban Development under [Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) (2011–2013) and has been a member of the [Expediency Discernment Council](/orgs/expediency-discernment-council).

nodes.label: Ali Shamkhani
nodes.role: Former SNSC Secretary (2013–2023); former Defense Minister (1997–2005)

data.role: Former SNSC Secretary (2013–2023); former Defense Minister (1997–2005)
data.bio: Ali Shamkhani is an ethnic Arab from Khuzestan who rose through the [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) Navy to become one of Iran's most senior security officials. He commanded the IRGC Navy during the Iran–Iraq War and later served as combined IRGC/Regular Navy commander. [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami) appointed him Defense Minister in 1997 — a moderate appointment widely seen as signalling detente — and he served for the full eight years. [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani) appointed him Secretary of the [Supreme National Security Council](/orgs/supreme-national-security-council) in 2013, where he served until 2023. He brokered the 2023 China-mediated Iran–Saudi Arabia normalisation agreement. He was replaced as SNSC Secretary by Ali Akbar Ahmadian in 2023.

nodes.label: Ali Younessi
nodes.role: Former Minister of Intelligence (1999–2005)

data.role: Former Minister of Intelligence (1999–2005)
data.bio: Ali Younessi served as Iran's Minister of Intelligence for most of [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami)'s presidency (1999–2005), replacing [Qorbanali Dorri-Najafabadi](/people/qorbanali-dorri-najafabadi) who resigned over the 1998 chain murders of dissidents. A cleric and jurist, Younessi was seen as a reformist appointment and took steps to investigate intelligence operations that had targeted journalists and intellectuals. He was later appointed as [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani)'s special assistant for ethnic minorities and religious affairs (2014–2021). In 2020 he was arrested on charges of espionage but released.

nodes.label: Alireza Arafi
nodes.role: Guardian Council member (2019–present); member of post-Khamenei interim leadership council

data.role: Guardian Council member (2019–present); member of post-Khamenei interim leadership council
data.bio: Alireza Arafi is a senior cleric and [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) member appointed by Khamenei in 2019. He has served as president of the Islamic Seminaries of Iran (Hawza). Following Khamenei's death in February 2026, he was appointed as the Guardian Council's representative on the three-member interim leadership council, alongside [President Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) and Chief Justice Mohseni-Ejei, pursuant to Article 111 of the constitution. He has been mentioned as a potential future Supreme Leader.

nodes.label: Alireza Tangsiri
nodes.role: Former Commander, IRGC Navy (2018–2026); killed in Israeli airstrike March 2026

data.role: Former Commander, IRGC Navy (2018–2026); killed in Israeli airstrike March 2026
data.bio: Alireza Tangsiri commanded the IRGC Navy from August 2018, personally appointed by [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) to replace [Ali Fadavi](/people/ali-fadavi). Born in a Persian Gulf fishing community, he built the IRGC Navy's 'swarm tactics' doctrine — coordinated attacks by hundreds of fast-attack craft and missiles designed to overwhelm US carrier groups. He was the public face of Iran's de facto blockade of the Strait of Hormuz during the 2026 Iran war. He was killed on 26 March 2026 in an Israeli airstrike at the port of Bandar Abbas, alongside other senior IRGC Navy commanders. The US Treasury had designated him a Specially Designated Global Terrorist in 2019.

nodes.label: Amin Hossein Rahimi
nodes.role: Minister of Justice (2021–present)

data.role: Minister of Justice (2021–present)
data.bio: Amin Hossein Rahimi has served as Iran's Minister of Justice since [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi)'s cabinet in 2021, retained by [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) in 2024. He is a career jurist who began working in Iran's judiciary at age 18. He served as Chief of the General and Revolutionary Courts in Tehran and as Political and Security Deputy of the Prosecutor General before his ministerial appointment. He is regarded as a loyalist of [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) and a product of the hardliner judicial network associated with [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi).

nodes.label: Amir Ali Hajizadeh
nodes.role: Former Commander, IRGC Aerospace Force (2009–2025); killed in Israeli airstrike June 2025

data.role: Former Commander, IRGC Aerospace Force (2009–2025); killed in Israeli airstrike June 2025
data.bio: Amir Ali Hajizadeh commanded the [IRGC Aerospace Force](/orgs/irgc-aerospace-force) from 2009 until his death in an Israeli airstrike on 13 June 2025, as part of the large-scale Israeli operation that also killed [Hossein Salami](/people/hossein-salami) and other senior commanders. He joined the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) in 1980 as a sniper during the Iran–Iraq War and was mentored by Hassan Tehrani Moghaddam — the 'Father of Iran's Missile Program' — who brought him into the nascent missile programme. He organised Iran's first missile unit 'Hadid' and rose to command Iran's entire ballistic missile and UAV arsenal. He directed the January 2020 missile strikes against US bases in Iraq following [Qasem Soleimani](/people/qasem-soleimani)'s assassination, and oversaw Iran's April and October 2024 direct missile and drone strikes against Israel. He was in command when IRGC missiles shot down Ukrainian flight PS752 on 8 January 2020, killing all 176 aboard — he admitted 'full responsibility' three days later. [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) awarded him the Order of Fath in October 2024 for the strikes on Israel.

nodes.label: Amir-Hossein Ghazizadeh Hashemi
nodes.role: First Deputy Speaker, 11th Majlis (2020–2024); 2021 presidential candidate

data.role: First Deputy Speaker, 11th Majlis (2020–2024); 2021 presidential candidate
data.bio: Amir-Hossein Ghazizadeh Hashemi is a hardline politician from Mashhad who served as First Deputy Speaker of the 11th [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) under Ghalibaf (2020–2024). He was one of only seven candidates approved by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) from 585 applicants in the 2021 presidential election, finishing fourth. A physician by training, he represents the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps)-aligned principlist networks that swept the 11th Majlis.

nodes.label: Amir-Hossein Sabeti
nodes.role: MP for Tehran, 12th Majlis (2024–present); former state television host

data.role: MP for Tehran, 12th Majlis (2024–present); former state television host
data.bio: Amir-Hossein Sabeti is a young conservative activist and state television anchor who placed second in the Tehran ballot in the 2024 parliamentary election, making him one of the most prominent new faces in the 12th [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly). Born in 1989, he represents the post-war generation of [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps)-aligned conservatives who entered politics via media rather than clerical routes. He is a member of the [Front of Islamic Revolution Stability](/orgs/front-of-islamic-revolution-stability). In 2012 he received a suspended sentence from a Revolutionary Court for protesting [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani)'s parliamentary policies on universities.

nodes.label: Ashraf Rabiei
nodes.role: Early MEK leader; first wife of Massoud Rajavi

data.role: Early MEK leader; first wife of Massoud Rajavi
data.bio: Ashraf Rabiei studied physics at Aryamehr University (now Sharif University) and joined the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq) in the early 1970s. She married [Massoud Rajavi](/people/massoud-rajavi) in 1980. In the 1980 parliamentary election, she received over 300,000 votes but did not win a seat. She was killed alongside [Moussa Khiabani](/people/moussa-khiabani) on 8 February 1982 when the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) raided their safehouse in Tehran. Camp Ashraf in Iraq was named in her honor.

nodes.label: Aziz Nasirzadeh
nodes.role: Minister of Defense (2024–present)

data.role: Minister of Defense (2024–present)
data.bio: Aziz Nasirzadeh is Iran's Minister of Defense under [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian), appointed in August 2024. A senior air force commander, he previously served as commander of the Iranian Air Force and as deputy chief of staff of the armed forces. His appointment to the defense portfolio followed the tenure of [Mohammad Reza Ashtiani](/people/mohammad-reza-ashtiani) under [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi).

nodes.label: Behzad Nabavi
nodes.role: First Deputy Speaker of Parliament, 6th Majlis (alternating 2000–2004); Co-founder, OMIIR

data.role: First Deputy Speaker of Parliament, 6th Majlis (alternating 2000–2004); Co-founder, OMIIR
data.bio: Behzad Nabavi is a senior figure of the Islamic left turned democratic reformist. An electrical engineer, he fought against the Shah and was imprisoned before the revolution. He co-founded the Islamic Revolution Committees (komiteh) and a forerunner to the Ministry of Intelligence. Under PM Mousavi he served as minister for executive affairs and minister of heavy industry. He was also Iran's chief negotiator in the 1981 [Algiers Accords](/events/algiers-accords-1981) that ended the hostage crisis. In the 6th [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) he alternated as First Deputy Speaker with [Mohammad-Reza Khatami](/people/mohammad-reza-khatami). He was arrested after the 2009 elections and sentenced to six years.

nodes.label: Bijan Namdar Zanganeh
nodes.role: Former Minister of Petroleum (1997–2005, 2013–2021); longest-serving Oil Minister in the Islamic Republic

data.role: Former Minister of Petroleum (1997–2005, 2013–2021); longest-serving Oil Minister in the Islamic Republic
data.bio: Bijan Namdar Zanganeh served as Iran's Minister of Petroleum under both [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami) (1997–2005) and [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani) (2013–2021) — twelve years in total, the longest oil ministerial tenure in the Islamic Republic's history. He is closely associated with the technocratic and pragmatist camp, and with Iran's efforts to attract foreign investment and maintain OPEC standing. His nomination by Rouhani was criticised by hardline newspapers. He is regarded as one of the most capable technocrats in Iranian economic history, having overseen Iran's oil expansion during the Khatami years and the post-JCPOA production recovery. He was replaced by [Javad Owji](/people/javad-owji) under [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi).

nodes.label: Ebrahim Raisi
nodes.role: 8th President of Iran (2021–2024)

data.role: 8th President of Iran (2021–2024)
data.bio: Ebrahim Raisi was a hardline cleric who served as head of the judiciary from 2019 before being elected president in 2021 in an election with record-low turnout. He is alleged to have been a member of the 'Death Commission' that ordered the mass execution of political prisoners in 1988. He was subject to US Treasury sanctions since 2019. His presidency was dominated by the Mahsa Amini protests of 2022. He died on 19 May 2024 when his helicopter crashed in foggy conditions near the Azerbaijan border.

nodes.label: Eskandar Momeni
nodes.role: Minister of Interior (2024–present)

data.role: Minister of Interior (2024–present)
data.bio: Eskandar Momeni is Iran's Minister of Interior under [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian), appointed in August 2024. A senior security official with a military background, he previously served in senior positions in law enforcement and security agencies. He superseded [Ahmad Vahidi](/people/ahmad-vahidi)'s hardline tenure at the Interior Ministry.

nodes.label: Esmaeil Khatib
nodes.role: Minister of Intelligence (2021–present)

data.role: Minister of Intelligence (2021–present)
data.bio: Esmaeil Khatib is Iran's Minister of Intelligence, appointed by [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) in 2021 and retained by [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) in 2024. He joined the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) intelligence wing in 1980 and built a career spanning the Ministry of Intelligence, the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) intelligence apparatus, and the [Office of the Supreme Leader](/orgs/office-of-the-supreme-leader). Under [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) as Judiciary chief, Khatib headed the Judiciary's Protection and Intelligence Centre. He studied jurisprudence under [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) himself, giving him direct personal ties to the Supreme Leader network. The Intelligence Ministry under Khatib has overseen intensified suppression of protest movements including the [2022 Mahsa Amini protests](/events/mahsa-amini-protests-2022).

nodes.label: Esmail Qaani
nodes.role: Commander of the IRGC Quds Force (2020–2025)

data.role: Commander of the IRGC Quds Force (2020–2025)
data.bio: Esmail Qaani succeeded [Qasem Soleimani](/people/qasem-soleimani) as [Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force) commander on the day of Soleimani's assassination, appointed immediately by Khamenei. A veteran of the [Iran-Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war) and longtime Quds Force deputy, his tenure was marked by criticism that he lacked Soleimani's strategic flair and public profile. He oversaw Quds Force operations during the Gaza conflict from 2023 and the broader escalation with Israel. Iranian sources reported he was killed in Israeli strikes on Iran in June 2025.

nodes.label: Gholam-Ali Haddad-Adel
nodes.role: Speaker of Parliament (2004–2008); Head of Iranian Academy of Persian Language and Literature

data.role: Speaker of Parliament (2004–2008); Head of Iranian Academy of Persian Language and Literature
data.bio: Gholam-Ali Haddad-Adel is a philosopher and politician who served as Speaker of Parliament from 2004 to 2008, the first non-cleric to hold the role since the 1979 revolution. He holds a PhD in philosophy from the University of Tehran and has led the Iranian [Academy of Persian Language and Literature](/orgs/iranian-academy-of-persian-language) since 1995. His daughter Zahra married [Mojtaba Khamenei](/people/mojtaba-khamenei), son of the Supreme Leader, making Haddad-Adel one of the most personally connected figures to the supreme leadership. He is a senior member of the principlist [Alliance of Builders of Islamic Iran](/orgs/alliance-of-builders-of-islamic-iran).

nodes.label: Gholam-Hossein Mohseni-Ejei
nodes.role: Chief Justice of Iran (2021–present); member of post-Khamenei interim leadership council

data.role: Chief Justice of Iran (2021–present); member of post-Khamenei interim leadership council
data.bio: Gholam-Hossein Mohseni-Ejei is a hardline jurist appointed Chief Justice by Khamenei in July 2021. He previously served as Minister of Intelligence under [Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) (2005–2009), as prosecutor-general, and as first deputy chief justice. He is regarded as a reliable enforcer of the regime's security priorities. Following Khamenei's death in February 2026, he became one of three members of the constitutional interim leadership council alongside [President Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) and [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) member Arafi.

nodes.label: Gholamreza Molabeygi
nodes.role: Jurist member of the Guardian Council

data.role: Jurist member of the Guardian Council
data.bio: Gholamreza Molabeygi is a jurist member of the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council), nominated by the Chief Justice and confirmed by the [Islamic Consultative Assembly](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly). He is one of six constitutional law experts on the Council.

nodes.label: Hadi Tahan Nazif
nodes.role: Spokesman of the Guardian Council; jurist member

data.role: Spokesman of the Guardian Council; jurist member
data.bio: Hadi Tahan Nazif is the current spokesman of the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) and a jurist member. He serves as the Council's primary public communicator, issuing formal statements on candidate vetting decisions and electoral results. He confirmed [Siamak Rahpeyk](/people/siamak-rahpeyk)'s reappointment as deputy secretary in July 2023.

nodes.label: Hamid Rasaee
nodes.role: MP for Tehran, 12th Majlis (2024–present); publisher, Noh Dey Weekly

data.role: MP for Tehran, 12th Majlis (2024–present); publisher, Noh Dey Weekly
data.bio: Hamid Rasaee is a hardline cleric representing Tehran in the [Islamic Consultative Assembly](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) (12th Majlis). He is a senior figure of the [Front of Islamic Revolution Stability](/orgs/front-of-islamic-revolution-stability) and publisher of the *Noh Dey Weekly*, shut down multiple times during the [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani) presidency. He publicly clashed in a famous 2015 parliamentary confrontation with an aide of [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) over the [JCPOA](/events/jcpoa-nuclear-deal-2015). He was previously disqualified by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) from one election cycle. He served in the 8th and 9th Majlis before returning in the 12th.

nodes.label: Hamid-Reza Haji Babaee
nodes.role: First Deputy Speaker, 12th Majlis (2024–present)

data.role: First Deputy Speaker, 12th Majlis (2024–present)
data.bio: Hamid-Reza Haji Babaee is a conservative politician from Hamadan Province serving as First Deputy Speaker of the 12th [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) under [Speaker Ghalibaf](/people/mohammad-bagher-ghalibaf). He previously served as Minister of Education under [Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) (2009–2013). A long-serving principlist MP, he represents the established traditional conservative wing of the Majlis.

nodes.label: Hassan Rouhani
nodes.role: 7th President of Iran (2013–2021)

data.role: 7th President of Iran (2013–2021)
data.bio: Hassan Rouhani was elected in 2013 on a centrist platform promising to ease sanctions. He oversaw negotiations that led to the 2015 [JCPOA](/events/jcpoa-nuclear-deal-2015) nuclear deal, the signature achievement of his presidency. After the United States withdrew from the JCPOA in 2018, Iran's economy deteriorated sharply. Prior to the presidency he served as Iran's chief nuclear negotiator (2003–2005) and as secretary of the [Supreme National Security Council](/orgs/supreme-national-security-council) for sixteen years.

nodes.label: Heydar Moslehi
nodes.role: Former Minister of Intelligence (2009–2013)

data.role: Former Minister of Intelligence (2009–2013)
data.bio: Heydar Moslehi served as Iran's Minister of Intelligence during [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad)'s second term (2009–2013). His appointment became the epicentre of the Ahmadinejad–Khamenei power struggle: Ahmadinejad attempted to dismiss Moslehi in 2011, but [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) publicly overruled the dismissal — prompting Ahmadinejad to boycott cabinet meetings for eleven days. This was the most visible public rupture in the Ahmadinejad–Khamenei relationship. A mid-ranking cleric, Moslehi had previously been head of the Organisation of Islamic Endowments and was an Ahmadinejad loyalist until the falling out.

nodes.label: Hossein-Ali Nayyeri
nodes.role: Head, Supreme Disciplinary Court for Judges (2013–present); former Sharia judge, Death Committee (1988)

data.role: Head, Supreme Disciplinary Court for Judges (2013–present); former Sharia judge, Death Committee (1988)
data.bio: Hossein-Ali Nayyeri presided over the Tehran 'Death Committee' that in summer 1988 ordered mass executions of political prisoners pursuant to [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini)'s fatwa — an event the UN has classified as crimes against humanity and genocide. He is explicitly named in Khomeini's written decree as the Sharia judge responsible for the Tehran operations. He personally told Ayatollah Montazeri on 15 August 1988 that 750 prisoners had already been executed in Tehran. The committee he chaired — alongside [Morteza Eshraqi](/people/morteza-eshraqi), [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) (deputy), and [Mostafa Pourmohammadi](/people/mostafa-pourmohammadi) (Intelligence Ministry representative) — interrogated and executed prisoners in minutes. He was subsequently appointed Deputy Chief Justice of Iran's Supreme Court (1989–2013) and has headed the Supreme Disciplinary Court for Judges since 2013, facing no legal consequences.

nodes.label: Hossein Amir-Abdollahian
nodes.role: Former Minister of Foreign Affairs (2021–2024); died in helicopter crash

data.role: Former Minister of Foreign Affairs (2021–2024); died in helicopter crash
data.bio: Hossein Amir-Abdollahian was Iran's Foreign Minister under [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) from August 2021 until his death in May 2024. He died alongside Raisi in the helicopter crash on 19 May 2024 in East Azerbaijan. A career diplomat who specialised in Arab and African affairs, he served as Deputy Foreign Minister for Arab and African Affairs under [Manouchehr Mottaki](/people/manouchehr-mottaki) (2011–2016) and was known for his close ties to Hezbollah, Hamas, and the [IRGC Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force). He was removed from his deputy role by [Mohammad Javad Zarif](/people/javad-zarif) in 2016, then became a senior adviser to [Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf](/people/mohammad-bagher-ghalibaf) at the Majlis. His foreign policy prioritised the 'resistance axis' and East-facing engagement with Russia and China.

nodes.label: Hossein Dehghan
nodes.role: Former Minister of Defense (2013–2017); IRGC Aerospace Force veteran

data.role: Former Minister of Defense (2013–2017); IRGC Aerospace Force veteran
data.bio: Hossein Dehghan is an IRGC brigadier general who served as Defense Minister in [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani)'s first cabinet (2013–2017). He joined the IRGC at its founding and commanded contingents in Lebanon and Syria in the early 1980s. He was Deputy Defense Minister under [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami). Under Rouhani's second term he was replaced by Amir Hatami. He ran as a presidential candidate in 2021. He is subject to US and EU sanctions. He was a military adviser to [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) before his cabinet appointment.

nodes.label: Hossein Salami
nodes.role: IRGC Commander-in-Chief (2019–2025)

data.role: IRGC Commander-in-Chief (2019–2025)
data.bio: Hossein Salami served as Commander-in-Chief of the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) from April 2019, appointed by Khamenei following the removal of [Mohammad Ali Jafari](/people/mohammad-ali-jafari). He joined the IRGC in 1980 at the outbreak of the [Iran-Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war) and rose through its Aerospace Force before appointment to the top post. He oversaw the IRGC during the Mahsa Amini crackdown of 2022, the expansion of Iran's ballistic missile and drone programmes, and the direct Iranian strikes against Israel in April 2024. He was reportedly killed in Israeli strikes on Iran in June 2025.

nodes.label: Hossein Taeb
nodes.role: Former head, IRGC Intelligence Organisation (2009–2022)

data.role: Former head, IRGC Intelligence Organisation (2009–2022)
data.bio: Hossein Taeb headed the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps)'s Intelligence Organisation for over thirteen years (2009–2022), making him one of the most feared figures in Iran's security apparatus. A student of [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) and wartime associate of [Mojtaba Khamenei](/people/mojtaba-khamenei) in the Habib Battalion, he combined IRGC and MOIS experience spanning from the [1988 massacre of political prisoners](/events/mass-executions-1988) through the [chain murders of 1998](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998) to the suppression of the [2009 Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) and [2022 Mahsa Amini protests](/events/mahsa-amini-protests-2022). He was dismissed in June 2022 following a series of Israeli intelligence successes inside Iran, including the assassination of nuclear scientists, and a foiled plot against Israeli tourists in Turkey that exposed IRGC-IO networks. He was replaced by General Mohammad Kazemi.

nodes.label: Javad Owji
nodes.role: Former Minister of Petroleum (2021–2024)

data.role: Former Minister of Petroleum (2021–2024)
data.bio: Javad Owji served as Iran's Minister of Petroleum under [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) from 2021 to 2024. A longtime executive in the oil and gas sector, he previously served as Deputy Oil Minister and managing director of the National Iranian Gas Company (NIGC). His appointment was influenced by First Vice President [Mohammad Mokhber](/people/mohammad-mokhber), given Owji's connections to Setad subsidiaries. He is sanctioned by the US Treasury. His predecessor [Bijan Namdar Zanganeh](/people/bijan-namdar-zanganeh) is regarded as one of Iran's most capable oil technocrats, a standard Owji found difficult to match. He was replaced by [Mohsen Paknejad](/people/mohsen-paknejad) under [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian).

nodes.label: Mohammad Javad Zarif
nodes.role: Minister of Foreign Affairs (2013–2021); Vice President (2024–2025)

data.role: Minister of Foreign Affairs (2013–2021); Vice President (2024–2025)
data.bio: Mohammad Javad Zarif served as Iran's Foreign Minister throughout [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani)'s presidency, leading the Iranian negotiating team that concluded the [JCPOA](/events/jcpoa-nuclear-deal-2015) nuclear deal in 2015. A US-educated career diplomat, he was Iran's ambassador to the United Nations before becoming foreign minister. He endorsed [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) in the 2024 presidential election and briefly served as vice president for strategic affairs before resigning. He was subject to US sanctions from 2019.

nodes.label: Kamal Kharrazi
nodes.role: Former Minister of Foreign Affairs (1997–2005); Strategic Council for Foreign Relations chairman

data.role: Former Minister of Foreign Affairs (1997–2005); Strategic Council for Foreign Relations chairman
data.bio: Kamal Kharrazi served as Iran's Foreign Minister for the full eight years of [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami)'s presidency (1997–2005). Previously Iran's Ambassador to the United Nations (1989–1997), he was a calm and relatively moderate voice on international affairs during the reform era, presiding over the diplomatic opening with Europe and the Middle East. He replaced [Ali Akbar Velayati](/people/ali-akbar-velayati) when Khatami took office. Since 2005 he has headed the Strategic Council for Foreign Relations, an advisory body to the Supreme Leader. His brother Mohsen Kharazi is connected by marriage to the Khamenei family.

nodes.label: Kazem Rajavi
nodes.role: Human rights advocate; Iran's first post-revolution ambassador to UN in Geneva

data.role: Human rights advocate; Iran's first post-revolution ambassador to UN in Geneva
data.bio: Kazem Rajavi was a professor of political science at the University of Geneva and elder brother of [Massoud Rajavi](/people/massoud-rajavi). He played a crucial role in saving Massoud's life by lobbying internationally to commute his death sentence under the Shah. After the 1979 revolution, he briefly served as Iran's ambassador to the UN in Geneva before breaking with the regime. He became a leading human rights advocate, documenting abuses by the Islamic Republic. On 24 April 1990, he was [assassinated](/events/assassination-of-kazem-rajavi-1990) near his home in Coppet, Switzerland by Iranian intelligence agents who had entered the country on diplomatic passports. [Ali Fallahian](/people/ali-fallahian) was charged by a Swiss court with masterminding the operation.

nodes.label: Kheyrollah Parvin
nodes.role: Jurist member of the Guardian Council

data.role: Jurist member of the Guardian Council
data.bio: Kheyrollah Parvin is a jurist member of the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council), nominated by the Chief Justice and confirmed by the [Islamic Consultative Assembly](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly). He is one of six constitutional law experts on the Council responsible for vetting legislation and candidates.

nodes.label: Mahmoud Ahmadinejad
nodes.role: 6th President of Iran (2005–2013)

data.role: 6th President of Iran (2005–2013)
data.bio: Mahmoud Ahmadinejad served as mayor of Tehran from 2003 before his presidential election victory in 2005. His two-term presidency was defined by confrontational nuclear rhetoric, Holocaust denial, economic mismanagement, and the disputed 2009 election that triggered the [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009). He significantly expanded the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps)'s economic and political power. After leaving office he fell out with Khamenei and was disqualified from subsequent presidential races.

nodes.label: Mahmoud Alavi
nodes.role: Former Minister of Intelligence (2013–2021)

data.role: Former Minister of Intelligence (2013–2021)
data.bio: Mahmoud Alavi served as Iran's Minister of Intelligence for both terms of [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani) (2013–2021). A cleric with a military background, he was previously head of the Political and Ideological Organisation of the Iranian Army — a post to which [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) had appointed him directly. He had also served as a deputy Defense Minister. As Intelligence Minister he navigated the tension between Rouhani's détente agenda and the hardline security establishment, and managed the period of the JCPOA negotiations and their aftermath.

nodes.label: Mahmoud Nabavian
nodes.role: MP for Tehran, 12th Majlis (2024–present); member, Front of Islamic Revolution Stability

data.role: MP for Tehran, 12th Majlis (2024–present); member, Front of Islamic Revolution Stability
data.bio: Mahmoud Nabavian is a Shia cleric who topped the Tehran ballot in the 2024 parliamentary election with the highest vote count in the capital. A student of [Mohammad-Taqi Mesbah Yazdi](/people/mohammad-taqi-mesbah-yazdi) and a senior member of the [Front of Islamic Revolution Stability](/orgs/front-of-islamic-revolution-stability), he has been a fierce opponent of Iran's nuclear diplomacy, comparing the [JCPOA](/events/jcpoa-nuclear-deal-2015) to Iran's humiliating 1828 territorial concessions to Russia. He attacked [Mohammad Javad Zarif](/people/javad-zarif) over the nuclear deal and was accused of lobbying against its revival in Vienna in 2022. He holds a PhD in philosophy from the [Imam Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) Educational and Research Institute. Previously served in the 9th [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) (2012–2016).

nodes.label: Manouchehr Mottaki
nodes.role: Former Foreign Minister (2005–2010); MP for Tehran, 12th Majlis (2024–present)

data.role: Former Foreign Minister (2005–2010); MP for Tehran, 12th Majlis (2024–present)
data.bio: Manouchehr Mottaki served as Foreign Minister under [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) from 2005 to 2010, when he was abruptly dismissed by text message while on a foreign visit to Senegal. A career diplomat, he previously served as ambassador to Turkey and Pakistan and as a member of earlier [Islamic Consultative Assembly](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) sessions. He ran for the 12th Majlis in 2024 heading the United Front principlist coalition list in Tehran.

nodes.label: Maryam Rajavi
nodes.role: President-elect of NCRI (1993–present); co-leader of MEK

data.role: President-elect of NCRI (1993–present); co-leader of MEK
data.bio: Maryam Rajavi studied metallurgy at Sharif University of Technology and joined the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq) after two of her sisters were killed — one by SAVAK and one executed by [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini)'s regime in 1982. She rose through MEK ranks, serving as joint leader (1985–1989) and Secretary General (1989–1993), before being elected President-elect of the [National Council of Resistance of Iran](/orgs/national-council-of-resistance-of-iran) in 1993. She married [Massoud Rajavi](/people/massoud-rajavi) in 1985. Under her leadership, women hold over half of senior positions in the organization. She is based in exile between France and Albania.

nodes.label: Masoud Pezeshkian
nodes.role: 9th President of Iran (2024–present)

data.role: 9th President of Iran (2024–present)
data.bio: Masoud Pezeshkian, a cardiac surgeon and long-serving MP for Tabriz, won the snap presidential election of July 2024 following Raisi's death. He ran on a moderate platform and was endorsed by reformist figures including [Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami) and [Zarif](/people/javad-zarif). Ethnically Azerbaijani, he is the first non-Persian-speaking president. He previously served as Minister of Health under Khatami (2001–2005).

nodes.label: Massoud Rajavi
nodes.role: Leader of MEK (1979–2003); co-founder of NCRI

data.role: Leader of MEK (1979–2003); co-founder of NCRI
data.bio: Massoud Rajavi joined the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq) in 1967 and worked directly with founder [Mohammad Hanifnejad](/people/mohammad-hanifnejad). Arrested and sentenced to death by the Shah's regime, his sentence was commuted to life imprisonment thanks to international pressure led by his brother [Kazem Rajavi](/people/kazem-rajavi). Released in January 1979 during the revolution, he became the MEK's undisputed leader. After the [June 1981 crackdown](/events/june-20-uprising-1981) he fled to Paris with [Abolhassan Banisadr](/people/abolhassan-banisadr), where he co-founded the [National Council of Resistance of Iran](/orgs/national-council-of-resistance-of-iran). Expelled from France in 1986, he relocated to Iraq and established Camp Ashraf under Saddam Hussein's patronage. He launched the disastrous [Operation Forough-e Javidan](/events/operation-forough-e-javidan-1988) in July 1988. He disappeared in March 2003 during the US invasion of Iraq and has not been publicly seen since.

nodes.label: Mehdi Karroubi
nodes.role: Speaker of Parliament (1989–1992, 2000–2004); Green Movement leader

data.role: Speaker of Parliament (1989–1992, 2000–2004); Green Movement leader
data.bio: Mehdi Karroubi served as Speaker of the [Islamic Consultative Assembly](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) twice. A presidential candidate in 2009, he refused to accept the disputed official results and became a co-leader of the [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) alongside [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi). He has been held under house arrest in Tehran since February 2011 without trial, one of the most prominent political detainees in the Islamic Republic.

nodes.label: Mehdi Shabzendedar Jahromi
nodes.role: Clerical member of the Guardian Council (reappointed 2022)

data.role: Clerical member of the Guardian Council (reappointed 2022)
data.bio: Mehdi Shabzendedar Jahromi is a clerical member of the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council), reappointed by [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) in July 2022 alongside [Mohammad-Reza Modarresi Yazdi](/people/mohammad-reza-modarresi-yazdi). He is one of the six faqih members directly appointed by the Supreme Leader to serve on the Council.

nodes.label: Mir-Hossein Mousavi
nodes.role: Last Prime Minister of Iran (1981–1989); Green Movement co-leader

data.role: Last Prime Minister of Iran (1981–1989); Green Movement co-leader
data.bio: Mir-Hossein Mousavi served as Iran's last prime minister, managing the economy through the entire [Iran-Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war). An architect and painter by training, he was a founding figure of the Islamic Republic before gradually moving into opposition. His disputed loss in the 2009 presidential election triggered the [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009). He has been under house arrest in Tehran since February 2011. In 2023 he called for a referendum to fundamentally change the constitution, a significant shift from his earlier reformist position. He is a distant relative of [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) through a shared ancestral line from Khameneh.

nodes.label: Mohammad Ali Jafari
nodes.role: Commander-in-Chief of the IRGC (2007–2019)

data.role: Commander-in-Chief of the IRGC (2007–2019)
data.bio: Mohammad Ali Jafari served as Commander-in-Chief of the [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) from 2007 to 2019. He reorganised the IRGC's command structure and expanded its internal security role, particularly the [Basij](/orgs/basij) militia. He oversaw IRGC operations during the Syrian civil war and the expansion of Iran's ballistic missile programme. He was replaced by [Hossein Salami](/people/hossein-salami) in April 2019.

nodes.label: Mohammad-Ali Rajai
nodes.role: 2nd President of the Islamic Republic of Iran (August 1981)

data.role: 2nd President of the Islamic Republic of Iran (August 1981)
data.bio: Mohammad-Ali Rajai was Iran's second president, serving for less than one month after his election on 24 July 1981. A committed revolutionary and former teacher, he had previously served as prime minister. He and his prime minister [Mohammad-Javad Bahonar](/people/mohammad-javad-bahonar) were assassinated in a bombing at the prime minister's office on 30 August 1981, an attack attributed to the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq) (MEK).

nodes.label: Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf
nodes.role: Speaker of Parliament (2020–present); Former Mayor of Tehran (2005–2017)

data.role: Speaker of Parliament (2020–present); Former Mayor of Tehran (2005–2017)
data.bio: Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf is a senior [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) figure who commanded the IRGC Aerospace Force and later the national police before serving as Mayor of Tehran for twelve years. He has run for president three times without success. Elected Speaker of Parliament in 2020 after the principlist sweep of that year's elections. He was one of 24 IRGC commanders who signed a 1999 letter threatening military action against student protesters, and has admitted to personally participating in street-level violence against them.

nodes.label: Mohammad Hanifnejad
nodes.role: Co-founder of Mojahedin-e Khalq (MEK)

data.role: Co-founder of Mojahedin-e Khalq (MEK)
data.bio: Mohammad Hanifnejad was an agricultural engineer and the principal co-founder of the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq) (MEK), established on 6 September 1965 alongside [Saeed Mohsen](/people/saeed-mohsen) and [Ali-Asghar Badizadegan](/people/ali-asghar-badizadegan). A former member of Mehdi Bazargan's Freedom Movement, he became convinced that a more radical approach was needed to challenge the Shah's regime. The three founders spent 1965–1969 recruiting cadres and developing the MEK's ideology, blending progressive Islam with anti-imperialist politics. He was arrested by SAVAK in 1971 and executed on 25 May 1972 along with his two co-founders.

nodes.label: Mohammad-Hassan Sadeghi Moghaddam
nodes.role: Jurist member of the Guardian Council; member, Central Committee for Election Supervision

data.role: Jurist member of the Guardian Council; member, Central Committee for Election Supervision
data.bio: Mohammad-Hassan Sadeghi Moghaddam is a jurist member of the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) and a member of its Central Committee for Election Supervision. He was sanctioned by the US Treasury in February 2020 alongside [Ahmad Jannati](/people/ahmad-jannati), [Siamak Rahpeyk](/people/siamak-rahpeyk), and [Abbas-Ali Kadkhodaei](/people/abbas-ali-kadkhodaei) for preventing free and fair parliamentary elections in Iran.

nodes.label: Mohammad-Javad Bahonar
nodes.role: Prime Minister of Iran (1981)

data.role: Prime Minister of Iran (1981)
data.bio: Mohammad-Javad Bahonar was a cleric and co-founder of the [Islamic Republican Party](/orgs/islamic-republican-party). He served briefly as prime minister under President [Mohammad-Ali Rajai](/people/mohammad-ali-rajai) in 1981. He and Rajai were assassinated in a bombing at the prime minister's office on 30 August 1981, an attack attributed to the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq) (MEK). He had previously served as Minister of Education.

nodes.label: Mohammad Khatami
nodes.role: 5th President of Iran (1997–2005)

data.role: 5th President of Iran (1997–2005)
data.bio: Mohammad Khatami was elected president in a landslide in 1997, winning around 70% of the vote on a platform of civil society, rule of law, and a Dialogue Among Civilizations. His two terms represented the peak of the reformist movement, marked by a free press and attempts to improve relations with the West. His reform agenda was consistently blocked by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) and judiciary.

nodes.label: Mohammad Mokhber
nodes.role: Former First Vice President (2021–2024); former acting President (May–July 2024)

data.role: Former First Vice President (2021–2024); former acting President (May–July 2024)
data.bio: Mohammad Mokhber served as [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi)'s First Vice President from 2021 and automatically became acting President upon Raisi's death on 19 May 2024, serving in that capacity until [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) was inaugurated in late July 2024. Before politics, Mokhber was the long-serving head of Setad Ejraiye Farmane Hazrat Emam (Setad), the vast conglomerate operating under the direct control of the [Office of the Supreme Leader](/orgs/office-of-the-supreme-leader), estimated to control assets worth tens of billions of dollars. His tenure at Setad made him one of Khamenei's most trusted operational executives. He is subject to US and EU sanctions.

nodes.label: Mohammad Reyshahri
nodes.role: Former Minister of Intelligence (1984–1989); head of Hajj and Pilgrimage Organisation

data.role: Former Minister of Intelligence (1984–1989); head of Hajj and Pilgrimage Organisation
data.bio: Mohammad Reyshahri was Iran's first dedicated Minister of Intelligence, serving under Prime Minister [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi) and President [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) from 1984 to 1989. He established the Ministry of Intelligence (MOIS) as an institutional entity and presided over it during the 1988 mass executions of political prisoners — the bloodiest period of internal repression in the Islamic Republic's history. He later headed the Hajj and Pilgrimage Organisation. He ran for president in 1997 but received less than 0.5% of the vote. He is regarded as a founding architect of Iran's security state.

nodes.label: Mohammad-Reza Aref
nodes.role: First Vice President (2024–present); former First Vice President (2013); former reformist presidential candidate

data.role: First Vice President (2024–present); former First Vice President (2013); former reformist presidential candidate
data.bio: Mohammad-Reza Aref is a reformist politician and electrical engineer who served briefly as First Vice President under [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani) (2013) before stepping aside at [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami)'s request to consolidate the reformist vote behind Rouhani in 2013. He had previously served as First Vice President under Khatami (2001–2005), where he oversaw technology and infrastructure policy. A former Minister of ICT under Khatami, he is now First Vice President under [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) (2024–present). He has been an MP for Tehran and heads the reformist parliamentary faction.

nodes.label: Mohammad Reza Ashtiani
nodes.role: Former Minister of Defense (2021–2024)

data.role: Former Minister of Defense (2021–2024)
data.bio: Mohammad Reza Ashtiani served as Minister of Defense under [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) from 2021 to 2024. A senior military officer, he previously served as Deputy Chief of Staff of the Iranian Armed Forces. He oversaw a period of accelerated missile and drone program development, including the April 2024 direct Iranian attack on Israel. He was replaced by [Aziz Nasirzadeh](/people/aziz-nasirzadeh) under [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian).

nodes.label: Mohammad-Reza Khatami
nodes.role: First Deputy Speaker of Parliament, 6th Majlis (2000–2004); Secretary-General, Islamic Iran Participation Front

data.role: First Deputy Speaker of Parliament, 6th Majlis (2000–2004); Secretary-General, Islamic Iran Participation Front
data.bio: Mohammad-Reza Khatami is a nephrologist and reformist politician, the younger brother of [President Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami). He entered politics after his brother's 1997 victory, becoming the top-polling candidate in Tehran in the March 2000 election. He served as Deputy Speaker of the reformist 6th [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) alternating with [Behzad Nabavi](/people/behzad-nabavi). He founded and served as Secretary-General of the [Islamic Iran Participation Front](/orgs/islamic-iran-participation-front), Iran's largest reformist party. He is married to Zahra Eshraghi, a granddaughter of [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini). He was permanently disqualified from running for office by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council).

nodes.label: Mohammad-Reza Modarresi Yazdi
nodes.role: Clerical member of the Guardian Council (reappointed 2022)

data.role: Clerical member of the Guardian Council (reappointed 2022)
data.bio: Mohammad-Reza Modarresi Yazdi is a clerical member of the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council), reappointed by [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) in July 2022 alongside Mehdi Shabzendedar Jahromi. A jurist from the Yazd clerical tradition, he is one of the six faqih members directly appointed by the Supreme Leader.

nodes.label: Mohammad-Taqi Mesbah Yazdi
nodes.role: Spiritual leader of the Front of Islamic Revolution Stability; co-founder of Haghani Seminary

data.role: Spiritual leader of the Front of Islamic Revolution Stability; co-founder of Haghani Seminary
data.bio: Mohammad-Taqi Mesbah Yazdi was the most influential ideologue of the Iranian ultra-right, described as 'a hardliner to terrify hardliners'. A student of [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) at the Qom seminary, he co-founded the [Haghani Seminary](/orgs/haghani-seminary) — the institution that trained the backbone of the Islamic Republic's clerical management class. He was the spiritual mentor of [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) and the guiding force behind the [Front of Islamic Revolution Stability](/orgs/front-of-islamic-revolution-stability), formally established in 2011 by his students. He endorsed Saeed Jalili in the 2013 presidential election and backed [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) in 2017. He repeatedly urged the [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) to suppress the reform movement by any means. He died on 1 January 2021.

nodes.label: Mohsen Armin
nodes.role: Reformist politician and journalist; member of the 6th Majlis

data.role: Reformist politician and journalist; member of the 6th Majlis
data.bio: Mohsen Armin is a reformist politician, journalist, and key figure in the [Mojahedin of the Islamic Revolution Organization](/orgs/mojahedin-of-islamic-revolution-organization). He served as a member of parliament in the reformist 6th [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly). He has been active in political journalism and the reform movement, advocating for political openness and democratic governance within the Islamic Republic framework.

nodes.label: Mohsen Fakhrizadeh
nodes.role: Head, IRGC Amad Project (nuclear weapons programme); IRGC Brigadier General; assassinated 2020

data.role: Head, IRGC Amad Project (nuclear weapons programme); IRGC Brigadier General; assassinated 2020
data.bio: Mohsen Fakhrizadeh was Iran's foremost nuclear weapons scientist and the head of the Amad Project — the Islamic Republic's clandestine nuclear weapons research programme, which the IAEA documented as pursuing warhead miniaturisation, implosion tests, and delivery system integration. He held the rank of Brigadier General in the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) and served simultaneously as Deputy Defence Minister for Research and Innovation. The IAEA identified him as the scientist 'orchestrating' Iran's military nuclear work in classified annex documents leaked in 2011. He was the target of [Amir Ali Hajizadeh](/people/amir-ali-hajizadeh)'s aerospace programme's delivery systems. He was assassinated on 27 November 2020 near Absard, east of Tehran, in a sophisticated remote-controlled ambush widely attributed to Israeli intelligence. His killing triggered calls in the Iranian Majlis to accelerate uranium enrichment and contributed to further JCPOA collapse. [Ali Akbar Salehi](/people/ali-akbar-salehi), then head of the [Atomic Energy Organisation of Iran](/orgs/atomic-energy-organisation-of-iran), described him as the 'shahid of science'.

nodes.label: Mohsen Paknejad
nodes.role: Minister of Petroleum (2024–present)

data.role: Minister of Petroleum (2024–present)
data.bio: Mohsen Paknejad is Iran's current Minister of Petroleum, appointed by [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) in August 2024. A petroleum engineer, he previously served in various management roles within the national oil sector, including at the National Iranian Oil Company (NIOC). His appointment was seen as a technocratic choice aimed at improving production efficiency and attracting investment to the oil sector.

nodes.label: Mojtaba Khamenei
nodes.role: 3rd Supreme Leader of the Islamic Republic of Iran (2026–present)

data.role: 3rd Supreme Leader of the Islamic Republic of Iran (2026–present)
data.bio: Mojtaba Khamenei is the second son of [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) and has been a shadowy but powerful figure in the Islamic Republic for decades. He long headed an intelligence bureau within the [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps), functioning as a key conduit between his father's office and the IRGC's security apparatus. His wife is Zahra Haddad-Adel, daughter of [Gholam-Ali Haddad-Adel](/people/gholam-ali-haddad-adel), former Speaker of the [Islamic Consultative Assembly](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly). Following Ali Khamenei's death on 28 February 2026, the [Assembly of Experts](/orgs/assembly-of-experts) elected Mojtaba as the third Supreme Leader on 8 March 2026 — the second succession in the republic's history.

nodes.label: Morteza Agha-Tehrani
nodes.role: MP; Chairman, Majlis Cultural Committee; former Secretary-General, Front of Islamic Revolution Stability

data.role: MP; Chairman, Majlis Cultural Committee; former Secretary-General, Front of Islamic Revolution Stability
data.bio: Morteza Agha-Tehrani is a senior figure of the [Front of Islamic Revolution Stability](/orgs/front-of-islamic-revolution-stability) who served as the party's founding Secretary-General before handing the role to [Sadegh Mahsouli](/people/sadegh-mahsouli) in late 2020. He chairs the [Islamic Consultative Assembly](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly)'s Cultural Committee — a key position that made him the primary legislative driver of the controversial mandatory hijab enforcement bill. He was also described as [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad)'s personal ethics teacher, cementing the Paydari network's ties to the Ahmadinejad presidency.

nodes.label: Morteza Eshraqi
nodes.role: Former Tehran Prosecutor General; member, Death Committee (1988)

data.role: Former Tehran Prosecutor General; member, Death Committee (1988)
data.bio: Morteza Eshraqi was Tehran's Prosecutor General in 1988 and is one of two officials explicitly named in [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini)'s fatwa as responsible for implementing the mass execution of political prisoners in Tehran — an event the UN classifies as crimes against humanity. His deputy, [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi), also sat on the committee. His presence at the committee's 15 August 1988 meeting with Ayatollah Montazeri is captured on audio recordings published in 2016. He subsequently served as a judge on the Supreme Court from 1989 to 1998.

nodes.label: Morteza Motahhari
nodes.role: Co-founder of Combatant Clergy Association; Council of Islamic Revolution chairman

data.role: Co-founder of Combatant Clergy Association; Council of Islamic Revolution chairman
data.bio: Morteza Motahhari was one of the most influential clerical scholars of the Islamic Revolution. A devoted student of [Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) in Qom, he co-founded the Hussainiyyah Ershad and the [Combatant Clergy Association](/orgs/combatant-clergy-association) (JRM). He served as chairman of Khomeini's Council of the Islamic Revolution in early 1979. He was assassinated on 1 May 1979 by the [Forqan Group](/orgs/forqan-group). His intellectual legacy underpins much of the Islamic Republic's ideological framework. He is the father-in-law of former [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) Speaker [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani) and the father of MP [Ali Motahari](/people/ali-motahari).

nodes.label: Mostafa Mohammad-Najjar
nodes.role: Former Minister of Defense (2005–2009); former Minister of Interior (2009–2013)

data.role: Former Minister of Defense (2005–2009); former Minister of Interior (2009–2013)
data.bio: Mostafa Mohammad-Najjar is an IRGC brigadier general who served as Defense Minister in [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad)'s first term (2005–2009) and then transferred to the Interior Ministry in the second term (2009–2013). As Defense Minister he presided over the rapid advancement of Iran's missile programme. As Interior Minister he supervised the disputed 2009 presidential election that triggered the [Green Movement protests](/events/green-movement-protests-2009). He is subject to US and EU sanctions.

nodes.label: Mostafa Pourmohammadi
nodes.role: Former Minister of Justice (2013–2017); former Minister of Interior (2005–2008)

data.role: Former Minister of Justice (2013–2017); former Minister of Interior (2005–2008)
data.bio: Mostafa Pourmohammadi is a cleric who served as Interior Minister under [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) (2005–2008) and later as Justice Minister under [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani) (2013–2017). His appointment by Rouhani was controversial and drew protests from human rights organisations, as Pourmohammadi had served as a member of the 'Death Committee' — the three-person panel in Tehran that approved the mass execution of thousands of political prisoners in 1988 following [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini)'s fatwa. He later explicitly defended the executions. He ran in the 2024 presidential election.

nodes.label: Mostafa Tajzadeh
nodes.role: Reformist politician; former Deputy Interior Minister

data.role: Reformist politician; former Deputy Interior Minister
data.bio: Mostafa Tajzadeh is a prominent reformist politician and member of the [Mojahedin of the Islamic Revolution Organization](/orgs/mojahedin-of-islamic-revolution-organization). He served as deputy interior minister under [President Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami). He was arrested after the 2009 [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) protests and sentenced to six years in prison. He attempted to run for president in 2021 but was disqualified by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council). He was re-arrested in 2022 during the Mahsa Amini protests.

nodes.label: Moussa Khiabani
nodes.role: Deputy leader of MEK; commander of MEK armed wing

data.role: Deputy leader of MEK; commander of MEK armed wing
data.bio: Moussa Khiabani studied physics at the University of Tehran and joined the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq). He trained in guerrilla warfare in Lebanon and was sentenced to life imprisonment in 1972 for MEK activities. Released in January 1979 during the revolution, he became [Massoud Rajavi](/people/massoud-rajavi)'s deputy and commanded the MEK's armed wing inside Iran. On 8 February 1982, the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) raided his safehouse in northern Tehran. After a three-hour firefight, Khiabani was killed along with [Ashraf Rabiei](/people/ashraf-rabiei).

nodes.label: Nasrollah Pejmanfar
nodes.role: MP; Chairman, Article 90 Committee, 11th and 12th Majlis

data.role: MP; Chairman, Article 90 Committee, 11th and 12th Majlis
data.bio: Nasrollah Pejmanfar is a hardline MP and member of the [Front of Islamic Revolution Stability](/orgs/front-of-islamic-revolution-stability) who chairs the [Islamic Consultative Assembly](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly)'s Article 90 Committee — the parliamentary oversight body that investigates complaints against the judiciary, executive, and Majlis itself. His committee chairmanship gives the Paydari Front an institutional oversight lever in disputes with other state bodies.

nodes.label: Qasem Soleimani
nodes.role: Commander of the IRGC Quds Force (1998–2020)

data.role: Commander of the IRGC Quds Force (1998–2020)
data.bio: Qasem Soleimani rose from a working-class background in Kerman to become the architect of Iran's network of regional proxies — [Hezbollah](/orgs/hezbollah), Shia militias in Iraq, the Houthis, and forces in Syria. As [Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force) commander from 1998 until his assassination, he was widely described as the most powerful military operative in the Middle East. He directed Iranian intervention in Syria from 2012 and was the primary coordinator of Iran's Axis of Resistance. He was killed on 3 January 2020 in a US drone strike at Baghdad International Airport, authorised by President Trump.

nodes.label: Qorbanali Dorri-Najafabadi
nodes.role: Former Minister of Intelligence (1997–1999); former Prosecutor General

data.role: Former Minister of Intelligence (1997–1999); former Prosecutor General
data.bio: Qorbanali Dorri-Najafabadi was [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami)'s first Intelligence Minister, appointed in 1997 as a compromise candidate after hardliners blocked Khatami's preferred choice. His tenure coincided with the 1998 'chain murders' — the serial killing of reformist intellectuals, writers, and political activists by rogue elements of the [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence). When evidence implicated the ministry itself, Dorri-Najafabadi resigned in 1999. He later served as Iran's Prosecutor General (2008–2009) and as a member of the [Assembly of Experts](/orgs/assembly-of-experts). He was a member of the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) before his intelligence appointment.

nodes.label: Rostam Ghasemi
nodes.role: Former Minister of Petroleum (2011–2013); former Minister of Roads (2021–2023)

data.role: Former Minister of Petroleum (2011–2013); former Minister of Roads (2021–2023)
data.bio: Rostam Ghasemi is an IRGC brigadier general and oil sector executive who served as Minister of Petroleum in [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad)'s second term (2011–2013) and then as Minister of Roads and Urban Development under [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) (2021–2023). He was previously managing director of the IRGC engineering arm Khatam al-Anbiya — the massive construction and contracting conglomerate that controls much of Iran's infrastructure market. He was replaced as Roads Minister in 2023. He is subject to US, EU, and UN sanctions.

nodes.label: Ruhollah Khomeini
nodes.role: Founder and first Supreme Leader of the Islamic Republic of Iran

data.role: Founder and first Supreme Leader of the Islamic Republic of Iran
data.bio: Ruhollah Khomeini was the Shia cleric and marja who led the 1979 [Iranian Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979), overthrowing the Pahlavi monarchy and establishing the Islamic Republic. He served as Supreme Leader from 1979 until his death in June 1989. He developed the doctrine of Velayat-e Faqih (Guardianship of the Islamic Jurist), which forms the constitutional basis of clerical rule in Iran.

nodes.label: Sadegh Larijani
nodes.role: Chairman of Expediency Discernment Council (2018–present); former Chief Justice (2009–2019); former Guardian Council member (2001–2021)

data.role: Chairman of Expediency Discernment Council (2018–present); former Chief Justice (2009–2019); former Guardian Council member (2001–2021)
data.bio: Sadegh Amoli Larijani is the younger brother of former Majlis Speaker [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani) and one of the most powerful figures in Iranian jurisprudence. He served as Chief Justice for a decade (2009–2019) and simultaneously sat on the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) from 2001. His tenure as Chief Justice was characterised by mass prosecutions of Green Movement figures, executions of political prisoners and juveniles, and widespread corruption allegations. He was removed from the judiciary in 2019 and replaced by [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi). He resigned from the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) in September 2021 in a public protest after the Council disqualified his brother [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani) from the presidential race. He was appointed chairman of the [Expediency Discernment Council](/orgs/expediency-discernment-council) by [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) in December 2018, replacing the late Mahmoud Hashemi Shahroudi.

nodes.label: Sadegh Mahsouli
nodes.role: Secretary-General, Front of Islamic Revolution Stability; former Interior Minister (2008–2009)

data.role: Secretary-General, Front of Islamic Revolution Stability; former Interior Minister (2008–2009)
data.bio: Sadegh Mahsouli is a former [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) commander and the organisational kingmaker within the [Front of Islamic Revolution Stability](/orgs/front-of-islamic-revolution-stability), described by Iranian media as the 'Godfather of Paydari'. He became Secretary-General in 2020, replacing [Morteza Agha-Tehrani](/people/morteza-agha-tehrani). A political associate of [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) from their time as officials in Azerbaijan Province, he served as Interior Minister (2008–2009) and Welfare Minister (2009–2010) under Ahmadinejad. He commands significant IRGC-adjacent organisational networks across provincial Iran.

nodes.label: Saeed Jalili
nodes.role: Secretary of the Supreme National Security Council (2007–2013); presidential candidate

data.role: Secretary of the Supreme National Security Council (2007–2013); presidential candidate
data.bio: Saeed Jalili is an Iranian hardline politician who served as secretary of the [Supreme National Security Council](/orgs/supreme-national-security-council) from 2007 to 2013, appointed by [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad).
