He was Iran's chief nuclear negotiator during that period, taking a confrontational stance toward Western powers. He ran unsuccessfully in the 2013 presidential election and was a leading candidate in the [2024 snap presidential election](/events/presidential-election-iran-2024-snap), finishing second to [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) in the runoff. He is closely aligned with the [Front of Islamic Revolution Stability](/orgs/front-of-islamic-revolution-stability) and was endorsed by [Mohammad-Taqi Mesbah-Yazdi](/people/mohammad-taqi-mesbah-yazdi). He lost part of his right leg during the [Iran–Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war).

nodes.label: Saeed Mohsen
nodes.role: Co-founder of Mojahedin-e Khalq (MEK)

data.role: Co-founder of Mojahedin-e Khalq (MEK)
data.bio: Saeed Mohsen was an engineer and one of the three co-founders of the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq), established on 6 September 1965 alongside [Mohammad Hanifnejad](/people/mohammad-hanifnejad) and [Ali-Asghar Badizadegan](/people/ali-asghar-badizadegan). A former member of the Freedom Movement, he helped build the MEK's early organizational structure. He was arrested by SAVAK in 1971 and executed on 25 May 1972.

nodes.label: Siamak Rahpeyk
nodes.role: Vice Secretary of the Guardian Council; jurist member

data.role: Vice Secretary of the Guardian Council; jurist member
data.bio: Siamak Rahpeyk is a jurist member of the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) and its Vice Secretary, responsible for overseeing the Council's executive and electoral affairs. Appointed to the position by Secretary [Ahmad Jannati](/people/ahmad-jannati), he has been the operational manager of Iran's candidate-vetting process. He publicly justified the mass disqualification of reformist candidates in the lead-up to the [2020 parliamentary election](/events/parliamentary-election-iran-2020). He was sanctioned by the US Treasury in February 2020 alongside [Ahmad Jannati](/people/ahmad-jannati), [Abbas-Ali Kadkhodaei](/people/abbas-ali-kadkhodaei), and others for denying Iranians free and fair elections.

nodes.label: Alliance of Builders of Islamic Iran

data.name_en: Alliance of Builders of Islamic Iran
data.description: The Alliance of Builders of Islamic Iran (Abadgaran) was a conservative-principlist political coalition formed in 2003. Described as 'Iran's neocons', it was composed mainly of [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) and [Basij](/orgs/basij) veterans who had been marginalised under [Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani) and [Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami). It swept the 2003 municipal and 2004 parliamentary elections — helped enormously by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council)'s mass disqualification of reformist candidates — and backed [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad)'s successful 2005 presidential run. [Gholam-Ali Haddad-Adel](/people/gholam-ali-haddad-adel), the [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) Speaker from 2004–2008, was a key figure.

The Assembly of Experts (Majles-e Khobregan-e Rahbari) is an 88-member body of Islamic scholars directly elected to eight-year terms by popular vote, with all candidates vetted by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council). It holds the sole constitutional authority to appoint, supervise, and if necessary dismiss the Supreme Leader. Sessions are held at least twice per year and all records are confidential. It has convened twice to appoint a Supreme Leader: in June 1989 choosing [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) after [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini)'s death, and on 8 March 2026 choosing [Mojtaba Khamenei](/people/mojtaba-khamenei). [Ahmad Jannati](/people/ahmad-jannati) served as its chairman from 2016 to 2024.

The Association of Combatant Clerics (Majma'-e Rowhaniyoun-e Mobarez, MRM) broke away from the conservative [Combatant Clergy Association](/orgs/combatant-clergy-association) in 1988. It is the reformist counterpart in clerical politics, associated with the Islamic left and later the reform movement. Key figures include [Mehdi Karroubi](/people/mehdi-karroubi) and [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami) (the president). It supports political liberalisation within an Islamic framework.

The Atomic Energy Organisation of Iran (AEOI, Sazman-e Enerji-ye Atomi-ye Iran) is the state body overseeing Iran's civil nuclear programme, including uranium enrichment, fuel fabrication, and the Bushehr nuclear power plant. Founded under the Shah in 1974, it continued after the revolution and became the primary institutional interface with the IAEA for inspections and safeguards. Its head is a Vice President of Iran. Key heads include Gholam Reza Aghazadeh (1997–2011), [Ali Akbar Salehi](/people/ali-akbar-salehi) (2009–2013, 2015–2021), and Mohammad Eslami (2021–present). The AEOI administers the enrichment facilities at Natanz and Fordow, the heavy water reactor at Arak, and the uranium conversion facility at Isfahan. It was the formal counterpart in [JCPOA](/events/jcpoa-nuclear-deal-2015) negotiations and continues enriching uranium to 60% purity under the collapsed JCPOA framework. The Amad Project — the clandestine nuclear weapons research programme led by [Mohsen Fakhrizadeh](/people/mohsen-fakhrizadeh) — operated in parallel to and largely hidden from the AEOI's formal structures.

The Basij Resistance Force (Niroo-ye Moqavemat-e Basij) is a paramilitary volunteer militia subordinate to the [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps). Founded by [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) in 1979, it played a prominent role in the [Iran-Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war) through human-wave assaults. It functions as an internal security and social control force, operating through mosques, universities, and government offices. It was centrally involved in the violent suppression of the [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) in 2009 and the Mahsa Amini protests in 2022.

The Combatant Clergy Association (Jame'e-ye Rowhaniyat-e Mobarez, JRM) is a conservative clerical political organisation founded in 1977 by a group of clerics including [Morteza Motahhari](/people/morteza-motahhari). It is one of the oldest and most powerful political bodies in the Islamic Republic, consistently aligning with the conservative and principlist factions. Key affiliated figures include [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani) and [Ali Akbar Nategh-Nouri](/people/ali-akbar-nategh-nouri). It split in 1988 when its left wing broke away to form the [Association of Combatant Clerics](/orgs/association-of-combatant-clerics) (MRM).

The Expediency Discernment Council of the System (Majma'-e Tashkhis-e Maslahat-e Nezam) was established by [Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) in 1988 to resolve legislative disputes between the [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) and [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council). It also acts as an advisory body to the Supreme Leader. Membership is appointed by the Supreme Leader and typically includes former presidents, senior clerics, and military figures, making it a powerful gathering point for Iran's political elite.

The Forqan Group (Goruh-e Forqan) was a small militant Islamist organisation that carried out a series of assassinations in the chaotic months following the 1979 revolution. It assassinated [Morteza Motahhari](/people/morteza-motahhari) on 1 May 1979 and attempted to kill [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani). The group opposed clerical rule, arguing for a return to the Quran without clerical intermediaries. Its members were arrested and executed by 1980.

The Front of Islamic Revolution Stability (Jebhe-ye Paydari-ye Enqelab-e Eslami), known as Paydari or the Steadfast Front, is the most hardline political faction operating within the Islamic Republic's official political space. Founded in 2011 by disciples of [Mohammad-Taqi Mesbah Yazdi](/people/mohammad-taqi-mesbah-yazdi), it advocates absolute velayat-e faqih and the total rejection of Western diplomacy. Key parliamentary figures include [Mahmoud Nabavian](/people/mahmoud-nabavian), [Hamid Rasaee](/people/hamid-rasaee), and [Amir-Hossein Sabeti](/people/amir-hossein-sabeti). The front outperformed [Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf](/people/mohammad-bagher-ghalibaf)'s SHANA coalition in Tehran in the 2024 parliamentary election.

The Guardian Council (Shura-ye Negahban) is a 12-member constitutional body with three mandates: vetting legislation for conformity with Islamic law and the constitution; supervising elections; and approving or disqualifying all candidates for parliament, presidency, and [Assembly of Experts](/orgs/assembly-of-experts). Six members are Islamic jurists appointed directly by the Supreme Leader; six are civil lawyers nominated by the Chief Justice (also appointed by the Supreme Leader) and confirmed by parliament. Members serve six-year terms in staggered cohorts.

The Haghani Seminary (Howzeh-ye Elmieh-ye Haqqani) is a hardline Shia seminary in Qom co-founded in the 1960s by [Mohammad-Taqi Mesbah Yazdi](/people/mohammad-taqi-mesbah-yazdi), Mohammad Beheshti, and [Ahmad Jannati](/people/ahmad-jannati). Described as 'a kind of École Nationale d'Administration for the Islamic Republic', its graduates form the backbone of the clerical management class in Iran's key political and security institutions. The seminary operates under a strict interpretation of velayat-e faqih and has been the primary incubator of the Haghani Circle ideology.

Hezbollah (Party of God) is a Lebanese Shia Islamist political party and militant group founded with the support of Iran's [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) during the Lebanese Civil War. It is the most powerful of Iran's regional proxies and receives extensive financial, military, and logistical support from the [IRGC Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force). It operates as both a political party in Lebanese parliament and a military force with an arsenal that surpasses the Lebanese Armed Forces.

Hussainiyyeh Ershad is a religious and cultural institution in Tehran co-founded by [Morteza Motahhari](/people/morteza-motahhari). It became famous in the 1960s and 1970s as a centre for modernist Islamic lectures, notably by Ali Shariati, whose talks attracted large audiences and contributed to the intellectual groundwork of the Islamic Revolution. It was closed by the Shah's government in 1973 but resumed activity after the revolution.

nodes.label: Academy of Persian Language and Literature

data.name_en: Academy of Persian Language and Literature
data.description: The Academy of Persian Language and Literature (Farhangestan-e Zaban va Adab-e Farsi) is Iran's official language-regulatory body, tasked with preserving and developing the Persian language. It coins Persian equivalents for foreign terms and oversees linguistic standards. [Gholam-Ali Haddad-Adel](/people/gholam-ali-haddad-adel) has served as its president since 1995.

The IRGC Aerospace Force (Niruy-e Hava-Faza-ye Sepah) is the branch of the [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) responsible for Iran's ballistic missile programme, satellite launches, and UAV (drone) operations. Founded in 1985 as the IRGC Air Force, it was expanded from 1989 onward under Supreme Leader [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei). Its role was elevated dramatically by the appointment of [Amir Ali Hajizadeh](/people/amir-ali-hajizadeh) as commander in 2009. The force operates Iran's arsenal of short-, medium-, and long-range ballistic missiles — including the Shahab, Emad, and Fattah-1 hypersonic series — as well as a large UAV fleet including the Shahed loitering munitions. It directed the January 2020 strikes on US bases, the April and October 2024 direct strikes on Israel, and the Strait of Hormuz drone campaign. It was responsible for the accidental shoot-down of Ukrainian Airlines PS752 in January 2020. [Hajizadeh](/people/amir-ali-hajizadeh) was killed in an Israeli airstrike in June 2025; he was succeeded by Hossein Mousavi Eftekhari.

The IRGC Intelligence Organisation (Sazman-e Ettelaat-e Sepah, IRGC-IO) is a parallel intelligence service operating alongside the civilian [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence). Formally restructured in its current form in 2009 following the [Green Movement protests](/events/green-movement-protests-2009), it handles counter-intelligence within the IRGC, surveillance of political dissidents, arrests of dual nationals and foreign journalists, and covert operations abroad. It is distinct from the IRGC Quds Force's external operations wing but overlaps in function. It was headed by [Hossein Taeb](/people/hossein-taeb) from 2009 to 2022, then by General Mohammad Kazemi. The organisation has been sanctioned by the US, EU, and UK for its role in arresting and torturing activists during the 2009, 2019, and 2022 protest waves.

The Quds Force (Jerusalem Force) is the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) branch responsible for extraterritorial operations, unconventional warfare, and support to non-state allies. It reports directly to the Supreme Leader. The Quds Force supports [Hezbollah](/orgs/hezbollah) in Lebanon, Hamas and Palestinian Islamic Jihad in Gaza, the Houthis in Yemen, and Shia militias in Iraq and Syria. It has been described by the US military as analogous to a combination of the CIA and JSOC.

The Islamic Consultative Assembly (Majles-e Shura-ye Eslami), commonly known as the Majlis or Majles, is the unicameral legislature of the Islamic Republic of Iran. It currently has 290 seats, elected for four-year terms. All candidates must be vetted and approved by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council). The Majlis can legislate, ratify treaties, and impeach the president, but all legislation must pass Guardian Council review for conformity with Islamic law and the constitution.

The Islamic Iran Participation Front (Jebhe-ye Mosharekat-e Iran-e Eslami), abbreviated IIPF, was the largest and most prominent reformist political party in post-revolutionary Iran. Founded in 1998 by [Mohammad-Reza Khatami](/people/mohammad-reza-khatami), it was the main vehicle for the reformist 2nd Khordad movement that swept the 6th [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) in 2000. The [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) barred all IIPF candidates from running in the 2004 parliamentary election, effectively eliminating the party from parliament. It was formally suspended by the government in 2010 following the [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) protests. The party advocated political pluralism, civil society, rule of law, and engagement with the West.

The Islamic Republican Party (Hezb-e Jomhuri-ye Eslami, IRP) was the dominant political party of the early Islamic Republic, co-founded in 1979 by [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini)'s inner circle including Mohammad Beheshti, [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani), [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei), and [Mohammad-Javad Bahonar](/people/mohammad-javad-bahonar). It dominated the first three [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) sessions and the executive branch until its dissolution in 1987 by Khomeini's order, following irreconcilable internal factional disputes. Its dissolution led directly to the formation of the rival [Combatant Clergy Association](/orgs/combatant-clergy-association) and [Association of Combatant Clerics](/orgs/association-of-combatant-clerics) as successor factions.

The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC), known domestically as Sepah or Pasdaran, is a multi-service elite military force established by [Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) in May 1979. It consists of five branches: Ground Forces, Aerospace Force, Navy, [Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force), and [Basij](/orgs/basij). Its commander-in-chief reports directly to the Supreme Leader. Beyond its military role, the IRGC controls a vast economic empire including Khatam al-Anbiya Construction, multiple banks, and extensive holdings in energy, telecommunications, and construction. Estimated at 125,000 personnel.

The Judiciary of Iran (Qovveh-ye Qaza'iyeh) is the judicial branch of the Islamic Republic, headed by a Chief Justice appointed directly by the [Office of the Supreme Leader](/orgs/office-of-the-supreme-leader) for five-year terms. The Chief Justice also nominates the six jurist members of the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council). The Judiciary has been a primary instrument of political repression, overseeing prosecutions of reformists, journalists, and human rights defenders. Chief Justices since 1989 include [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani) (briefly), Mohammad Yazdi (1989–1999), Mahmoud Hashemi Shahroudi (1999–2009), [Sadegh Amoli Larijani](/people/sadegh-larijani) (2009–2019), [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) (2019–2021), and [Gholam-Hossein Mohseni-Ejei](/people/gholam-hossein-mohseni-ejei) (2021–present).

Khatam al-Anbiya Construction Headquarters is the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps)'s engineering and construction arm and one of Iran's largest conglomerates. Originally formed to rebuild infrastructure after the [Iran-Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war), it grew into a sprawling enterprise with interests in oil and gas, transportation, water management, and construction. It has been subject to US and EU sanctions for its role in Iran's nuclear and missile programmes and its ties to the IRGC.

nodes.label: Ministry of Defense and Armed Forces Logistics

data.name_en: Ministry of Defense and Armed Forces Logistics
data.name_fa: وزارت دفاع و پشتیبانی نیروهای مسلح
data.description: Iran's Defense Ministry (Vezarat-e Defa va Poshtibani-ye Niruhaye Mosallah) manages defense procurement, arms production, and logistics for Iran's armed forces. It does not command operational forces; that function resides with the General Staff and IRGC command. Key ministers: [Ali Shamkhani](/people/ali-shamkhani) (1997–2005), [Mostafa Mohammad-Najjar](/people/mostafa-mohammad-najjar) (2005–2009), [Ahmad Vahidi](/people/ahmad-vahidi) (2009–2013), [Hossein Dehghan](/people/hossein-dehghan) (2013–2017), Amir Hatami (2017–2021), [Mohammad Reza Ashtiani](/people/mohammad-reza-ashtiani) (2021–2024), [Aziz Nasirzadeh](/people/aziz-nasirzadeh) (2024–present).

nodes.label: Ministry of Economic Affairs and Finance

data.name_en: Ministry of Economic Affairs and Finance
data.description: Iran's Ministry of Economic Affairs and Finance (Vezarat-e Omur-e Eqtesadi va Daraii) manages the state budget, tax policy, financial regulation, and oversees state-owned enterprises. Key ministers: Ali Tayyebnia (2013–2017), Masoud Karbasian (2017–2018), Farhad Dejpasand (2018–2021), Ehsan Khandouzi (2021–2024), [Abdolnaser Hemmati](/people/abdolnaser-hemmati) (2024, briefly).

Iran's Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Vezarat-e Omur-e Khareje) is the executive body responsible for conducting the country's foreign policy, managing diplomatic missions, and negotiating international agreements. In practice, strategic foreign policy is set by the [Office of the Supreme Leader](/orgs/office-of-the-supreme-leader) and coordinated through the [Supreme National Security Council](/orgs/supreme-national-security-council), with the Ministry implementing day-to-day diplomacy. Foreign Ministers since 1981: [Ali Akbar Velayati](/people/ali-akbar-velayati) (1981–1997), [Kamal Kharrazi](/people/kamal-kharrazi) (1997–2005), [Manouchehr Mottaki](/people/manouchehr-mottaki) (2005–2010), [Ali Akbar Salehi](/people/ali-akbar-salehi) (2010–2013), [Mohammad Javad Zarif](/people/javad-zarif) (2013–2021), [Hossein Amir-Abdollahian](/people/hossein-amir-abdollahian) (2021–2024), [Abbas Araghchi](/people/abbas-araghchi) (2024–present).

The Ministry of Intelligence (Vezarat-e Ettelaat, VAJA / formerly MOIS) is Iran's primary civilian intelligence service, established in 1983 from several post-revolution intelligence bodies. The Minister of Intelligence must be a qualified Islamic jurist (mojtahed). The Supreme Leader has historically exercised direct influence over this appointment. Ministers: Mohammad Reyshahri (1984–1989), [Ali Fallahian](/people/ali-fallahian) (1989–1997), [Qorbanali Dorri-Najafabadi](/people/qorbanali-dorri-najafabadi) (1997–1999), Ali Younessi (1999–2005), [Gholam-Hossein Mohseni-Ejei](/people/gholam-hossein-mohseni-ejei) (2005–2009), [Heydar Moslehi](/people/heydar-moslehi) (2009–2013), [Mahmoud Alavi](/people/mahmoud-alavi) (2013–2021), [Esmaeil Khatib](/people/esmaeil-khatib) (2021–present).

Iran's Ministry of Interior (Vezarat-e Keshvar) oversees provincial governance, domestic security coordination, civil registration, and the administration of elections. As the agency that supervises and certifies election results, it wields enormous political power in conjunction with the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council). Key ministers: Ali Akbar Natiq-Nouri (1981–1985), Mohammad Reyshahri briefly (1989), [Ali Fallahian](/people/ali-fallahian) briefly, Abdollah Nouri (1989–1993, 1997–1998), [Mostafa Pourmohammadi](/people/mostafa-pourmohammadi) (2005–2008), [Sadegh Mahsouli](/people/sadegh-mahsouli) (2008–2009), Mostafa Mohammad-Najjar (2009–2013), Abdolreza Rahmani Fazli (2013–2017), [Ahmad Vahidi](/people/ahmad-vahidi) (2021–2024), [Eskandar Momeni](/people/eskandar-momeni) (2024–present).

Iran's Ministry of Justice (Vezarat-e Dadgostari) manages the administrative interface between the executive and judiciary. The minister does not head the Judiciary — the Chief Justice is separately appointed by the Supreme Leader — but administers courts, prisons, and judicial coordination. Key ministers: [Mostafa Pourmohammadi](/people/mostafa-pourmohammadi) (2013–2017, implicated in 1988 massacre as deputy minister), Alireza Avaei (2017–2021, sanctioned for executions), [Amin Hossein Rahimi](/people/amin-hossein-rahimi) (2021–2024), Amin Hossein Rahimi (2024–present, reappointed by Pezeshkian).

Iran's Ministry of Petroleum (Vezarat-e Naft) manages the National Iranian Oil Company (NIOC) and oversees all upstream and downstream oil, gas, and petrochemical activities. Oil revenue has historically funded the majority of the state budget. Key ministers: Gholam Reza Aghazadeh (1985–1997), [Bijan Namdar Zanganeh](/people/bijan-namdar-zanganeh) (1997–2005, 2013–2021), Kazem Vaziri Hamaneh (2005–2008), Gholamhossein Nozari (2008–2009), Masoud Mir-Kazemi (2009–2011), [Rostam Ghasemi](/people/rostam-ghasemi) (2011–2013), [Javad Owji](/people/javad-owji) (2021–2024), [Mohsen Paknejad](/people/mohsen-paknejad) (2024–present).

The Mojahedin-e Khalq (MEK), also known as the People's Mojahedin Organization of Iran (PMOI), was co-founded in 1965 by [Mohammad Hanifnejad](/people/mohammad-hanifnejad), [Saeed Mohsen](/people/saeed-mohsen), and [Ali-Asghar Badizadegan](/people/ali-asghar-badizadegan), blending progressive Islam with anti-imperialist politics. After the three founders were executed by the Shah in 1972, [Massoud Rajavi](/people/massoud-rajavi) emerged as leader upon his release in 1979. The MEK participated in the revolution but clashed with [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini)'s regime, organizing the [June 20 uprising](/events/june-20-uprising-1981) and carrying out the 1981 bombing that killed President [Mohammad-Ali Rajai](/people/mohammad-ali-rajai) and Prime Minister [Mohammad-Javad Bahonar](/people/mohammad-javad-bahonar). After a brutal crackdown, the MEK relocated to Iraq under Saddam Hussein's patronage and founded the [National Liberation Army](/orgs/national-liberation-army-of-iran), whose catastrophic [Operation Forough-e Javidan](/events/operation-forough-e-javidan-1988) in 1988 provided a pretext for the [mass executions](/events/mass-executions-1988) of thousands of prisoners. The [NCRI](/orgs/national-council-of-resistance-of-iran), led by [Maryam Rajavi](/people/maryam-rajavi), serves as its political umbrella. The MEK's intelligence network [revealed Iran's secret Natanz nuclear facility](/events/natanz-nuclear-revelation-2002) in 2002. Now based in Albania, it was removed from the US terrorist list in 2012.

nodes.label: Mojahedin of the Islamic Revolution Organization

data.name_en: Mojahedin of the Islamic Revolution Organization
data.description: The Mojahedin of the Islamic Revolution Organization (OMIIR) is a reformist party reconstituted in 1991 from the left wing of the original 1979 organisation of the same name. It is closely allied with the [Islamic Iran Participation Front](/orgs/islamic-iran-participation-front) within the reform movement. Key figures include [Behzad Nabavi](/people/behzad-nabavi), [Mohsen Armin](/people/mohsen-armin), and [Mostafa Tajzadeh](/people/mostafa-tajzadeh). The party emphasised the republican dimensions of the Islamic Republic over its theocratic elements. Several members were arrested and imprisoned after the 2009 [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) protests.

nodes.label: National Council of Resistance of Iran

data.name_en: National Council of Resistance of Iran
data.description: The NCRI was co-founded by [Massoud Rajavi](/people/massoud-rajavi) and President [Abolhassan Banisadr](/people/abolhassan-banisadr) on 21 July 1981 as a broad coalition opposing the Islamic Republic. It functions as a parliament-in-exile comprising approximately 540 members, with the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq) as its principal member organization. [Maryam Rajavi](/people/maryam-rajavi) has served as its President-elect since 1993. Banisadr left the coalition in 1984 over disagreements with Rajavi's leadership. The NCRI is headquartered at Auvers-sur-Oise near Paris and advocates for the overthrow of the Islamic Republic and establishment of a secular democratic government.

The National Liberation Army of Iran (NLA) was the armed wing of the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq), founded in 1987 in Iraq with Saddam Hussein's support. It fielded approximately 7,000 fighters equipped with Iraqi-supplied weapons and armored vehicles. Its most significant operation was [Operation Forough-e Javidan](/events/operation-forough-e-javidan-1988) on 26 July 1988, a failed offensive into western Iran that was repelled by Iranian forces with devastating losses. The NLA was effectively disbanded after the 2003 US invasion of Iraq when its members were disarmed by American forces.

The National Trust Party (Hezb-e E'temad-e Melli) was founded in 2005 by [Mehdi Karroubi](/people/mehdi-karroubi) after he came third in the first round of the presidential election that year. It positioned itself as a reformist but pragmatic party advocating social freedoms, economic reform, and political pluralism. Karroubi ran for president again under the party's banner in 2009. After the disputed election result and the [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009), the party's leadership was suppressed and Karroubi himself was placed under house arrest.

The Office of Prime Minister was the head of government position in the Islamic Republic from 1979 to 1989. Under the 1979 constitution, executive power was divided between the president and the prime minister. The role was abolished in the 1989 constitutional revision, which merged its powers into the presidency. [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi) was the last holder of the office.

The Presidency of the Islamic Republic of Iran is the second-highest office in the political system, below the Supreme Leader. The president is elected by popular vote for four-year terms, with a two-term consecutive limit. The president chairs the cabinet and is responsible for implementing the constitution. The post of prime minister was abolished in the 1989 constitutional revision, merging its powers into the presidency.

The Office of the Supreme Leader (Daftar-e Maqam-e Mo'azzam-e Rahbari) is the institutional apparatus of the Supreme Leader of Iran. The Supreme Leader holds ultimate authority over all branches of government, the armed forces, the judiciary, and state media under the doctrine of Velayat-e Faqih. The office encompasses numerous subsidiary bodies including the Supreme Leader's Inspection Office, his representatives in the provinces, and his representatives to the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) and other military branches.

The Popular Mobilization Forces (PMF, or Hashd al-Sha'abi) is an Iraqi state-sponsored umbrella organisation of predominantly Shia paramilitary groups, many of which receive training, funding, and direction from Iran's [IRGC Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force). Its deputy commander Abu Mahdi al-Muhandis was killed alongside [Qasem Soleimani](/people/qasem-soleimani) in the January 2020 US drone strike at Baghdad International Airport.

The Supreme National Security Council (Shora-ye Ali-ye Amniat-e Melli, SNSC) was established by the 1989 constitutional revision to coordinate Iran's defence, intelligence, and foreign policy. The President chairs it; the Secretary — appointed with the Supreme Leader's approval — is effectively Iran's national security adviser and a direct representative of the [Office of the Supreme Leader](/orgs/office-of-the-supreme-leader). The SNSC has also been Iran's primary nuclear negotiating body. Key secretaries include [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani) (1989–2005), [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani) (2005–2007), and Saeed Jalili (2007–2013).

data.name: Algiers Accords
data.description: The Algiers Accords were agreements between Iran and the United States, mediated by Algeria, that resolved the Iran hostage crisis and secured the release of 52 American embassy staff held in Tehran for 444 days. [Behzad Nabavi](/people/behzad-nabavi) served as Iran's chief negotiator. The accords were signed on 19 January 1981, and the hostages were released on 20 January, minutes after Ronald Reagan's inauguration as US president.

data.name: Assassination of Kazem Rajavi
data.description: On 24 April 1990, [Kazem Rajavi](/people/kazem-rajavi) — professor of political science at the University of Geneva, human rights advocate, and elder brother of MEK leader [Massoud Rajavi](/people/massoud-rajavi) — was assassinated near his home in Coppet, Switzerland. He was shot at close range by multiple [Iranian intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence) agents who had entered Switzerland on diplomatic passports. [Ali Fallahian](/people/ali-fallahian), then intelligence minister under [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani), was charged by a Swiss court with masterminding the operation. In 2020, the Swiss case was reopened to be investigated as a crime against humanity.

data.name: Chain Murders of Iranian Intellectuals (1998)
data.description: Between late 1998 and early 1999, a series of murders of Iranian intellectuals, writers, and political activists — dubbed the 'chain murders' (ghatlha-ye zanjire'i) — were carried out by agents of the [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence) under [Ali Fallahian](/people/ali-fallahian)'s successors. Key victims included political couple Dariush and Parvaneh Foruhar (stabbed in their home, November 1998), writer Mohammad Mokhtari (strangled, December 1998), and translator Mohammad Jafar Pouyandeh (strangled, December 1998). The killings were eventually traced to a rogue unit within the Ministry of Intelligence, led by MOIS deputy Saeed Emami, who later died in custody (officially 'suicide'). Intelligence Minister [Qorbanali Dorri-Najafabadi](/people/qorbanali-dorri-najafabadi) resigned. [Hossein Taeb](/people/hossein-taeb) is reported to have been involved in the chain murders through his MOIS counter-intelligence role under [Ali Fallahian](/people/ali-fallahian). The murders severely damaged [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami)'s reformist credibility and exposed the existence of rogue killing units within the security services.

data.name: 1989 Constitutional Revision
data.description: The 1989 constitutional revision amended the Constitution of the Islamic Republic shortly before [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini)'s death. Key changes included abolishing the position of prime minister and merging its powers into the presidency, establishing the [Supreme National Security Council](/orgs/supreme-national-security-council) and the [Expediency Discernment Council](/orgs/expediency-discernment-council) as constitutional bodies, removing the requirement that the Supreme Leader be a marja (senior religious authority), and expanding the Supreme Leader's powers. The revision was approved by referendum on 28 July 1989, the same day as the presidential election that brought [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani) to office.

data.name: Assassination of Mohsen Fakhrizadeh
data.description: Mohsen Fakhrizadeh, head of the IRGC's clandestine Amad nuclear weapons research programme and deputy Defence Minister, was assassinated on 27 November 2020 near Absard, east of Tehran. A sophisticated remote-controlled ambush used a weapon mounted on a truck to target his vehicle. Iran and Western intelligence attributed the killing to Israeli intelligence (Mossad), though Israel did not officially claim responsibility. Fakhrizadeh had been identified by the IAEA as the central figure in Iran's military nuclear work. His death was the most high-profile in a series of targeted killings of Iranian nuclear scientists and defence officials attributed to Israel since 2010. The Majlis responded by passing legislation requiring the government to increase enrichment to 20% and restrict IAEA inspector access — accelerating the nuclear programme and hastening the JCPOA's collapse. [Ali Akbar Salehi](/people/ali-akbar-salehi) at the [Atomic Energy Organisation of Iran](/orgs/atomic-energy-organisation-of-iran) described him as the 'shahid of science'.

data.name: Green Movement Protests
data.description: Following the disputed June 2009 presidential election in which incumbent [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) was declared the winner over reformist candidates [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi) and [Mehdi Karroubi](/people/mehdi-karroubi), millions of Iranians took to the streets in the largest protests since the 1979 revolution. The government response was violent; the [Basij](/orgs/basij) and [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) killed dozens of protesters. The movement was suppressed by late 2009, and its leaders Mousavi and Karroubi were placed under house arrest in 2011.

data.name: Iran-Iraq War
data.description: The Iran-Iraq War (1980–1988) was an eight-year conflict initiated by Iraq's invasion of Iran in September 1980. It was the longest conventional war of the twentieth century, killing an estimated 500,000 to 1 million people on both sides. The war shaped a generation of Iranian military and political leaders, particularly those who went on to lead the [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps). [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi) served as prime minister throughout the conflict. The war ended with UN-brokered ceasefire Resolution 598, which [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) accepted, comparing it to 'drinking poison'.

data.name: Iranian Revolution
data.description: The Islamic Revolution of 1979 overthrew the Pahlavi monarchy and established the Islamic Republic of Iran. Following months of mass protests, Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi fled Iran on 16 January 1979. [Ayatollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) returned from exile on 1 February 1979. The revolutionary government took power on 11 February 1979, the date now commemorated as the Day of Victory of the Islamic Revolution. A constitutional referendum in March confirmed the Islamic Republic, and a new theocratic constitution was approved in December 1979.

nodes.label: Israeli Strikes on Iran — Operation Rising Lion (June 2025)

data.name: Israeli Strikes on Iran — Operation Rising Lion (June 2025)
data.description: On 13 June 2025, Israel launched large-scale airstrikes on Iran targeting military command centres, nuclear infrastructure, and senior IRGC leadership — an operation Israel called 'Operation Rising Lion'. The strikes, described as pre-emptive, killed approximately 30 IRGC generals in the opening attack, including [Hossein Salami](/people/hossein-salami) (IRGC Commander-in-Chief), [Amir Ali Hajizadeh](/people/amir-ali-hajizadeh) (Aerospace Force commander), [Esmail Qaani](/people/esmail-qaani) (Quds Force commander), and Mohammad Bagheri (Chief of Staff of the Armed Forces). US airstrikes simultaneously damaged nuclear facilities at Natanz, Isfahan, and Fordow. The strikes initiated what became known as the 'Twelve-Day War'. [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) was killed in a subsequent strike on 28 February 2026, triggering the [succession crisis](/events/mojtaba-khamenei-supreme-leader-2026) that resulted in [Mojtaba Khamenei](/people/mojtaba-khamenei) becoming Supreme Leader.

data.name: JCPOA Nuclear Deal
data.description: The Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action (JCPOA) was finalised in Vienna on 14 July 2015 between Iran and the P5+1 (US, UK, France, Russia, China, Germany) plus the EU. After 20 months of negotiations, Iran agreed to limit uranium enrichment to 3.67%, reduce its centrifuge count to 5,060 at Natanz, repurpose Fordow for non-enrichment research, and redesign the Arak heavy water reactor to eliminate plutonium production — all in exchange for lifting of UN, EU, and US nuclear-related sanctions. [Mohammad Javad Zarif](/people/javad-zarif) led Iran's negotiating team alongside [Abbas Araghchi](/people/abbas-araghchi) and [Ali Akbar Salehi](/people/ali-akbar-salehi). [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani) championed the deal domestically against fierce hardliner opposition led by [Saeed Jalili](/people/saeed-jalili) and [Mahmoud Nabavian](/people/mahmoud-nabavian). The deal went into effect on 16 January 2016 ('Implementation Day') after IAEA verification. It was publicly opposed by Israel and Saudi Arabia.

data.name: US Withdrawal from JCPOA
data.description: On 8 May 2018, President Donald Trump announced US withdrawal from the JCPOA, reimposing all sanctions and adding new ones under a 'maximum pressure' campaign. The US re-designated Iran's oil, banking, shipping, and petrochemical sectors. The withdrawal triggered a cascade: European signatories (UK, France, Germany) attempted to sustain the deal through the Instex mechanism, but US secondary sanctions deterred European businesses from trading with Iran. Iran responded by gradually stepping down its JCPOA commitments from May 2019 onward, enriching uranium above the 3.67% cap, exceeding the stockpile limit, and eventually restricting IAEA inspector access. By January 2020, following [Qasem Soleimani](/people/qasem-soleimani)'s assassination, Iran announced it would no longer observe any JCPOA limits. The deal's collapse significantly accelerated Iran's uranium enrichment programme, which reached 60% purity by 2021 — a short technical step from the 90% weapons-grade level.

data.name: June 20 Uprising (30 Khordad)
data.description: On 20 June 1981, the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq) organized massive anti-government protests in Tehran and other cities in response to the impeachment of President [Abolhassan Banisadr](/people/abolhassan-banisadr). The Tehran demonstration attracted up to 500,000 people. Leading clerics declared demonstrators "enemies of God" subject to immediate execution. The [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) violently suppressed the protests, killing approximately 50 people near Tehran University alone and arresting over 1,000. At least 23 arrested protesters, including teenage girls, were subsequently executed. This date marks the beginning of the MEK's open war with the Islamic Republic and the start of the 1981–1982 mass repressions that drove the organization underground and eventually into exile.

data.name: Assassination of Ali Larijani
data.description: Former [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) Speaker and senior establishment figure [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani) was killed in an Israeli airstrike on Tehran on 17 March 2026. His son Morteza Larijani and office chief Alireza Bayat were also killed in the attack. The killing came weeks after the death of [Supreme Leader Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) and amid an ongoing Israeli-Iranian conflict that had severely degraded Iran's military and nuclear infrastructure. Iran officially declared Larijani a martyr. The assassination removed one of the Islamic Republic's most experienced diplomatic and legislative figures at a critical moment of regime transition.

data.name: Mahsa Amini Protests (Woman, Life, Freedom)
data.description: Following the death of 22-year-old Mahsa (Zhina) Amini in morality police custody on 16 September 2022, nationwide protests erupted under the slogan Woman, Life, Freedom (Zan, Zendegi, Azadi). The protests were the most sustained unrest in the history of the Islamic Republic, continuing for months across all provinces. Security forces killed over 500 protesters according to human rights organisations. Over 15,000 people were arrested. The protests were characterised by women publicly removing and burning their headscarves.

data.name: 1988 Mass Executions of Political Prisoners
data.description: In late July 1988, [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) issued a secret fatwa ordering the execution of political prisoners across Iran who remained loyal to the Mojahedin-e Khalq (MEK/PMOI) or refused to renounce their political beliefs. Three-person 'Death Commissions' were established in prisons throughout Iran, each comprising a Sharia judge, a prosecutor, and an intelligence representative. In Tehran, the commission — chaired by [Hossein-Ali Nayyeri](/people/hossein-ali-nayyeri), with [Morteza Eshraqi](/people/morteza-eshraqi) as prosecutor, [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) as deputy prosecutor, and [Mostafa Pourmohammadi](/people/mostafa-pourmohammadi) as Intelligence Ministry representative — processed prisoners in minutes. Estimated deaths range from 2,800 to 5,000 (UN, HRW, Amnesty) or higher. The victims were buried in secret mass graves. Ayatollah Montazeri, then designated successor to Khomeini, protested and was subsequently dismissed as heir. In 2016 his family released an audio recording of his 15 August 1988 meeting with Death Committee members in which he said: 'The greatest crime committed during the reign of the Islamic Republic... has been committed by you.' The UN Special Rapporteur on Iran concluded in a 2024 report that the executions constituted crimes against humanity and genocide. None of the perpetrators has faced legal accountability.

nodes.label: Election of Mojtaba Khamenei as Supreme Leader

data.name: Election of Mojtaba Khamenei as Supreme Leader
data.description: On 8 March 2026, the [Assembly of Experts](/orgs/assembly-of-experts) elected [Mojtaba Khamenei](/people/mojtaba-khamenei) — second son of the late [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) — as the third Supreme Leader of the Islamic Republic, eight days after his father's death on 28 February 2026. The election confirmed a dynastic transfer of power. An interim three-member leadership council — [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian), [Gholam-Hossein Mohseni-Ejei](/people/gholam-hossein-mohseni-ejei), and [Alireza Arafi](/people/alireza-arafi) — had governed under Article 111 of the constitution in the intervening period. Mojtaba Khamenei, like his father in 1989, did not hold the marja' credential traditionally associated with the Supreme Leader role.

data.name: Natanz Nuclear Revelation
data.description: On 14 August 2002, [NCRI](/orgs/national-council-of-resistance-of-iran) spokesman Alireza Jafarzadeh held a press conference in Washington revealing the existence of two secret Iranian nuclear sites: the Natanz uranium enrichment facility and the Arak heavy water production facility, based on intelligence from the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq)'s network inside Iran. This revelation triggered the first IAEA inspections of Iranian nuclear sites in 2003, which confirmed traces of enriched uranium despite Iranian decontamination efforts. The disclosure ultimately led to six UN Security Council resolutions against Iran and set in motion the international confrontation over Iran's nuclear program that culminated in the [JCPOA](/events/jcpoa-nuclear-deal-2015).

data.name: Operation Forough-e Javidan (Eternal Light)
data.description: On 26 July 1988, six days after [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) accepted UN Resolution 598 for a ceasefire in the [Iran–Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war), the MEK's [National Liberation Army](/orgs/national-liberation-army-of-iran) launched Operation Forough-e Javidan (Eternal Light) from Iraqi territory with approximately 7,000 fighters. The offensive aimed to capture Kermanshah and march on Tehran. MEK forces initially seized the town of Islamabad-e Gharb but were met with a devastating Iranian counterattack (Operation Mersad). The MEK suffered catastrophic losses — 1,500 to 2,500 killed in three days — and the operation failed completely. This was the last land battle of the Iran–Iraq War and is widely considered to have provided the regime a pretext for the [1988 mass executions](/events/mass-executions-1988) of political prisoners.

data.name: 2000 Iranian Parliamentary Election (6th Majlis)
data.description: The February 2000 parliamentary election produced a landslide victory for the reformist coalition aligned with [President Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami). Reformist parties, led by the [Islamic Iran Participation Front](/orgs/islamic-iran-participation-front), won approximately 195 of 290 seats. It was the high-water mark of the reform movement: the Islamic Iran Participation Front's [Mohammad-Reza Khatami](/people/mohammad-reza-khatami) topped the Tehran ballot with nearly 1.8 million votes. The 6th [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly), led by [Speaker Mehdi Karroubi](/people/mehdi-karroubi), was the most reformist parliament in the Islamic Republic's history and was in constant conflict with the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council).

data.name: 2004 Iranian Parliamentary Election (7th Majlis)
data.description: The February 2004 parliamentary election was marked by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council)'s mass disqualification of approximately 2,400 candidates, including most reformist incumbents and all candidates of the [Islamic Iran Participation Front](/orgs/islamic-iran-participation-front). Approximately 80 sitting MPs were barred from seeking re-election. The result was a sweeping conservative victory, with the [Abadgaran](/orgs/alliance-of-builders-of-islamic-iran) coalition winning over 190 seats. Speaker [Gholam-Ali Haddad-Adel](/people/gholam-ali-haddad-adel) was elected with 226 of 259 votes, becoming the first non-clerical Speaker since the revolution. Turnout collapsed to around 51%.

data.name: 2020 Iranian Parliamentary Election (11th Majlis)
data.description: The February 2020 parliamentary election produced another conservative sweep, with the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) disqualifying over 9,000 candidates including many moderate incumbents. Turnout was a record low of approximately 42% nationally and around 25% in Tehran. The principlist coalition won a large majority, and [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps)-aligned figure [Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf](/people/mohammad-bagher-ghalibaf) was elected Speaker days later. The election represented the full consolidation of hardliner control over the legislature.

data.name: 2024 Iranian Parliamentary Election (12th Majlis)
data.description: The March 2024 parliamentary election produced Iran's lowest-ever turnout at approximately 41% nationally and below 25% in Tehran. The [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) approved only around 15,200 of 48,847 registered candidates to run. Principlists won 233 of 290 seats. In Tehran, ultraconservatives from the [Front of Islamic Revolution Stability](/orgs/front-of-islamic-revolution-stability) outperformed the establishment SHANA coalition backed by Speaker [Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf](/people/mohammad-bagher-ghalibaf). [Mahmoud Nabavian](/people/mahmoud-nabavian) topped the Tehran ballot, followed by [Amir-Hossein Sabeti](/people/amir-hossein-sabeti) and [Hamid Rasaee](/people/hamid-rasaee). [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) won a Tabriz seat but resigned it in July 2024 upon election as President. A second round was held on 10 May 2024.

data.name: 1997 Iranian Presidential Election
data.description: The 1997 Iranian presidential election resulted in a landmark victory for reformist candidate [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami), who won approximately 70% of the vote against establishment favourite [Ali Akbar Nategh-Nouri](/people/ali-akbar-nategh-nouri). The election shocked the Iranian political establishment and is considered the beginning of the reformist era. It demonstrated the significant gap between popular preferences and the ruling establishment's expectations.

data.name: 2005 Iranian Presidential Election
data.description: The 2005 presidential election resulted in the surprise victory of Tehran mayor [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) over [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani) in a run-off. Ahmadinejad ran as a populist outsider against the establishment, backed by the [Alliance of Builders of Islamic Iran](/orgs/alliance-of-builders-of-islamic-iran) and networks within the [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) and [Basij](/orgs/basij). [Mehdi Karroubi](/people/mehdi-karroubi), who initially led in first-round results before official tallies shifted, alleged fraud. The election marked the beginning of a sharp turn toward hardline populism.

data.name: 2009 Iranian Presidential Election
data.description: The June 2009 presidential election was one of the most consequential and disputed in the Islamic Republic's history. Incumbent [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) was declared the winner with 63% of the vote over reformist challenger [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi) and [Mehdi Karroubi](/people/mehdi-karroubi). The opposition alleged massive fraud, and the disputed results triggered the [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) — the largest protests Iran had seen since the 1979 revolution. The [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) certified the results after a partial recount.

data.name: 2021 Iranian Presidential Election
data.description: The June 2021 presidential election produced one of the lowest turnouts in the Islamic Republic's history — approximately 48% nationally, and around 26% in Tehran. The [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) disqualified 585 of 592 candidates who registered, approving only seven to run. The disqualifications included former Majlis Speaker [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani), former president [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad), and First Vice President Eshaq Jahangiri. [Saeed Jalili](/people/saeed-jalili) withdrew before election day to consolidate the hardliner vote. [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) won with approximately 62% of votes cast. The disqualification of [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani) prompted his brother [Sadegh Amoli Larijani](/people/sadegh-larijani) to resign from the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) in protest.

data.name: 2024 Iranian Snap Presidential Election
data.description: The snap presidential election was triggered by [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi)'s death in a helicopter crash on 19 May 2024. The first round was held on 28 June 2024; with no outright majority, a runoff on 5 July 2024 saw [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) defeat Saeed Jalili with nearly 16.4 million votes to Jalili's approximately 13.5 million. The result surprised many analysts who had expected a Jalili victory. Reformist consolidation behind Pezeshkian — led by endorsements from [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami) and senior figures — proved decisive. Jalili was backed by the [Front of Islamic Revolution Stability](/orgs/front-of-islamic-revolution-stability) and had close ties to [Mojtaba Khamenei](/people/mojtaba-khamenei).

nodes.label: Death of President Raisi in Helicopter Crash

data.name: Death of President Raisi in Helicopter Crash
data.description: President [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi), Foreign Minister [Hossein Amir-Abdollahian](/people/hossein-amir-abdollahian), and six others died when their Bell 212 helicopter crashed in a fog-covered mountainous area near Jolfa in East Azerbaijan Province on 19 May 2024. The crash triggered a constitutional provision requiring a snap presidential election within fifty days. First Vice President [Mohammad Mokhber](/people/mohammad-mokhber) served as acting president. [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) was elected as successor on 5 July 2024.

data.name: Assassination of Qasem Soleimani
data.description: [Qasem Soleimani](/people/qasem-soleimani), commander of the [IRGC Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force), was killed in a US drone strike on his convoy outside Baghdad International Airport on 3 January 2020, authorised by President Trump. Also killed was Abu Mahdi al-Muhandis, deputy commander of Iraq's [Popular Mobilization Forces](/orgs/popular-mobilization-forces). Iran vowed retaliation and launched ballistic missile strikes on US bases in Iraq days later. [Esmail Qaani](/people/esmail-qaani) was appointed Quds Force commander the same day.
